([/ˌɪndiəˈnæpəlɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Idianapolis.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Idianapolis.wav"), [*IN-dee-ə-NAP-ə-lis*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) colloquially known as **Indy**, is the [capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") and [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Indiana "List of municipalities in Indiana") of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Marion County](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana"). Indianapolis is situated in the state's central [till plain](/wiki/Till_plain "Till plain") region along the west fork of the [White River](/wiki/White_River_(Indiana) "White River (Indiana)"). The city's official slogan, "[Crossroads of America](/wiki/Crossroads_of_America "Crossroads of America")", reflects its historic importance as a transportation hub and its relative proximity to other major North American markets.[[12]](#cite_note-Indy_Chamber-12)[[13]](#cite_note-NPS-13)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Capital and most populous city of Indiana, United States

This article is about the capital city of the U.S. state of Indiana. For other uses, see [Indianapolis (disambiguation)](/wiki/Indianapolis_(disambiguation) "Indianapolis (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Indianópolis](/wiki/Indian%C3%B3polis "Indianópolis") or [Marion County, Indiana](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana").

**Indianapolis** ([/ˌɪndiəˈnæpəlɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Idianapolis.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Idianapolis.wav"), [*IN-dee-ə-NAP-ə-lis*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")),[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) colloquially known as **Indy**, is the [capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") and [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Indiana "List of municipalities in Indiana") of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Marion County](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana"). Indianapolis is situated in the state's central [till plain](/wiki/Till_plain "Till plain") region along the west fork of the [White River](/wiki/White_River_(Indiana) "White River (Indiana)"). The city's official slogan, "[Crossroads of America](/wiki/Crossroads_of_America "Crossroads of America")", reflects its historic importance as a transportation hub and its relative proximity to other major North American markets.[[12]](#cite_note-Indy_Chamber-12)[[13]](#cite_note-NPS-13)

According to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the [Indianapolis proper](/wiki/Indianapolis_(balance) "Indianapolis (balance)") had a population of 887,642.[[14]](#cite_note-QuickFacts22-14) Indianapolis is the [16th-most populous](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") city in the United States and the fourth-most populous state capital. In the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), it ranks third among cities, after [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio"). The [Indianapolis metropolitan area](/wiki/Indianapolis_metropolitan_area "Indianapolis metropolitan area") is the [33rd-most populous](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") metropolitan statistical area in the United States, home to 2.1 million residents.[[15]](#cite_note-IBRC_MSA-15) With a population of more than 2.6 million, the combined statistical area [ranks 28th](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area").[[16]](#cite_note-IBRC_CSA-16) Indianapolis proper covers 368 square miles (950 square kilometers), making it the [18th-most extensive](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_area "List of United States cities by area") city by land area in the country.

[Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") inhabited the area dating to as early as 10,000 BC.[[17]](#cite_note-Jones-17) Under the Treaty of St. Mary's (1818), both the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") and the [Miami Nation](/wiki/Miami_Nation "Miami Nation") relinquished their tribal lands in central Indiana. The Miami Nation of Indiana (and its branch, the Miami Nation of Oklahoma) held primary claim to the territory, which forms part of Cession 99.[[18]](#cite_note-Ency-18) In 1821, Indianapolis was established as a [planned city](/wiki/Planned_city "Planned city") for the new seat of Indiana's state government. The city was [platted](/wiki/Plat "Plat") by [Alexander Ralston](/wiki/Alexander_Ralston "Alexander Ralston") and [Elias Pym Fordham](/wiki/Elias_Pym_Fordham "Elias Pym Fordham") on a one-square-mile (2.6 km2) grid. Completion of the [National](/wiki/National_Road "National Road") and [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Road "Michigan Road") roads and later arrival of rail solidified the city's position as a major manufacturing and commercial center.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Since the 1970 [city-county consolidation](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county"), known as [Unigov](/wiki/Unigov "Unigov"), local government administration operates under the direction of an elected 25-member [city-county council](/wiki/Indianapolis_City-County_Council "Indianapolis City-County Council") headed by the [mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Indianapolis "List of mayors of Indianapolis").

Indianapolis anchors the [28th largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._metropolitan_areas_by_GDP "List of U.S. metropolitan areas by GDP") metropolitan economy in the United States.[[20]](#cite_note-FRED-20) Prominent industries include trade, transportation, and utilities; education and health services; professional and business services; government; leisure and hospitality; and manufacturing.[[21]](#cite_note-industries-21) The city has notable [niche markets](/wiki/Niche_market "Niche market") in [amateur sports](/wiki/Amateur_sports "Amateur sports") and [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing").[[22]](#cite_note-employment_industries-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Naptown-23) As of 2019, Indianapolis has been home to two [major league](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") sports teams, three [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies, eight university campuses, and numerous cultural institutions, including [the world's largest children's museum](/wiki/The_Children%27s_Museum_of_Indianapolis "The Children's Museum of Indianapolis").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) The city's international reputation rests primarily on the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500"), the world's largest single-day sporting event.[[26]](#cite_note-Keefer-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Davidson-27) Among the city's [historic sites and districts](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Marion_County,_Indiana "National Register of Historic Places listings in Marion County, Indiana"), Indianapolis is home to the largest collection of monuments dedicated to veterans and war casualties in the United States outside of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[28]](#cite_note-MemorialsMonuments-28)[[29]](#cite_note-IWM-29)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

See also: [List of modern words formed from Greek polis](/wiki/List_of_modern_words_formed_from_Greek_polis "List of modern words formed from Greek polis")

The name "Indianapolis" is derived from pairing the state's name, *Indiana* (meaning "Land of the Indians", or simply "Indian Land"[[30]](#cite_note-30)), with the suffix **-polis**, the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") word for "city". [Jeremiah Sullivan](/wiki/Jeremiah_Sullivan "Jeremiah Sullivan"), justice of the [Indiana Supreme Court](/wiki/Indiana_Supreme_Court "Indiana Supreme Court"), is credited with coining the name.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Other names considered were Concord, Delaware, Suwarrow, [Tecumseh](/wiki/Tecumseh "Tecumseh"), Wayne, and Whetzel.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Indianapolis](/wiki/History_of_Indianapolis "History of Indianapolis")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Indianapolis](/wiki/Timeline_of_Indianapolis "Timeline of Indianapolis").

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Greater_Indianapolis_%3Bthe_history%2C_the_industries%2C_the_institutions%2C_and_the_people_of_a_city_of_homes_%281910%29_%2814803447463%29.jpg/250px-Greater_Indianapolis_%3Bthe_history%2C_the_industries%2C_the_institutions%2C_and_the_people_of_a_city_of_homes_%281910%29_%2814803447463%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greater_Indianapolis_;the_history,_the_industries,_the_institutions,_and_the_people_of_a_city_of_homes_(1910)_(14803447463).jpg)

An 1820 illustration of Indianapolis

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Restored_plat_of_Indianapolis.png/250px-Restored_plat_of_Indianapolis.png)](/wiki/File:Restored_plat_of_Indianapolis.png)

[Alexander Ralston](/wiki/Alexander_Ralston "Alexander Ralston")'s "Plat of the Town of Indianapolis" in 1821

In 1816, the year Indiana gained statehood, [U.S. Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") designated four sections of land in central Indiana as the future seat of state government, contingent on tribal removal.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Under the [Treaty of St. Mary's](/wiki/Treaty_of_St._Mary%27s "Treaty of St. Mary's") (1818), the Delaware and Miami nations relinquished title to these designated lands, with agreement to vacate by 1821.[[18]](#cite_note-Ency-18) This tract of land, which was called the [New Purchase](/wiki/New_Purchase_(1818) "New Purchase (1818)"), included the site selected for the new state capital in 1820.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The indigenous people of the land prior to [systematic removal](/wiki/Indian_removals_in_Indiana "Indian removals in Indiana") are the [Miami Nation of Indiana](/wiki/Miami_Nation_of_Indiana "Miami Nation of Indiana") ([Miami Nation of Oklahoma](/wiki/Miami_Nation_of_Oklahoma "Miami Nation of Oklahoma")) and Indianapolis makes up part of Cession 99; the primary treaty between the indigenous population and the United States was the [Treaty of St. Mary's](/wiki/Treaty_of_St._Mary%27s_(1818) "Treaty of St. Mary's (1818)") in 1818.[[36]](#cite_note-Madison123-36)

The availability of new federal lands for purchase in central Indiana attracted settlers, many of them descendants of families from northwestern Europe. Although many of these first European and American settlers were [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants"), a large proportion of the early [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") and [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") immigrants were [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics"). Few [African Americans](/wiki/African_American "African American") lived in central Indiana before 1840.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The first [European Americans](/wiki/European_American "European American") to permanently settle in the area that became Indianapolis were either the McCormick or Pogue families. The McCormicks are generally considered to be the first permanent settlers; however, some historians believe [George Pogue](/wiki/George_Pogue "George Pogue") and family may have arrived first, on March 2, 1819, and settled in a log cabin along the creek that was later called [Pogue's Run](/wiki/Pogue%27s_Run "Pogue's Run"). Other historians have argued as early as 1822 that [John Wesley McCormick](/wiki/John_Wesley_McCormick "John Wesley McCormick") and his family and employees became the area's first European American settlers, settling near the [White River](/wiki/White_River_(Indiana) "White River (Indiana)") in February 1820.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

On January 11, 1820, the [Indiana General Assembly](/wiki/Indiana_General_Assembly "Indiana General Assembly") authorized a committee to select a site in central Indiana for the new state capital.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The state legislature approved the site, adopting the name Indianapolis on January 6, 1821.[[1]](#cite_note-founding-1) In April, [Alexander Ralston](/wiki/Alexander_Ralston "Alexander Ralston") and [Elias Pym Fordham](/wiki/Elias_Pym_Fordham "Elias Pym Fordham") were appointed to survey and design a town plan for the new settlement.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Indianapolis became a seat of county government on December 31, 1821, when [Marion County](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana"), was established. A combined county and town government continued until 1832 when Indianapolis was incorporated as a town.[[41]](#cite_note-The_Encyclopedia_of_Indianapolis-41)

Indianapolis became an incorporated city effective March 30, 1847. [Samuel Henderson](/wiki/Samuel_Henderson_(Indianapolis_mayor) "Samuel Henderson (Indianapolis mayor)"), the city's first mayor, led the new city government, which included a seven-member city council. In 1853, voters approved a new city charter that provided for an elected mayor and a fourteen-member city council. The city charter continued to be revised as Indianapolis expanded.[[41]](#cite_note-The_Encyclopedia_of_Indianapolis-41) Effective January 1, 1825, the seat of state government moved to Indianapolis from [Corydon, Indiana](/wiki/Corydon,_Indiana "Corydon, Indiana"). In addition to state government offices, a [U.S. district court](/wiki/United_States_district_court "United States district court") was established at Indianapolis in 1825.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Growth occurred with the opening of the [National Road](/wiki/National_Road "National Road") through the town in 1827, the first major federally funded highway in the United States.[[43]](#cite_note-Hyman34-Baer11-43) A small segment of the ultimately failed [Indiana Central Canal](/wiki/Indiana_Central_Canal "Indiana Central Canal") was opened in 1839.[[44]](#cite_note-Canal-44) The first railroad to serve Indianapolis, the [Jeffersonville, Madison and Indianapolis Railroad](/wiki/Jeffersonville,_Madison_and_Indianapolis_Railroad "Jeffersonville, Madison and Indianapolis Railroad"), began operation in 1847, and subsequent railroad connections fostered growth.[[45]](#cite_note-Britannica-45) [Indianapolis Union Station](/wiki/Indianapolis_Union_Station "Indianapolis Union Station") was the first of its kind in the world when it opened in 1853.[[46]](#cite_note-Union_Station-46)

#### American Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: American Civil War")]

Main article: [Indianapolis in the American Civil War](/wiki/Indianapolis_in_the_American_Civil_War "Indianapolis in the American Civil War")

During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), Indianapolis was mostly loyal to the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") cause. [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Indiana "Governor of Indiana") [Oliver P. Morton](/wiki/Oliver_P._Morton "Oliver P. Morton"), a major supporter of [President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), quickly made Indianapolis a rallying place for [Union army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") troops. On February 11, 1861, [President-elect](/wiki/President-elect_of_the_United_States "President-elect of the United States") Lincoln arrived in the city, en route to [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") for his [presidential inauguration](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Abraham_Lincoln "First inauguration of Abraham Lincoln"), marking the first visit from a president-elect in the city's history.[[47]](#cite_note-47) On April 16, 1861, the first orders were issued to form Indiana's first regiments and establish Indianapolis as a headquarters for the state's volunteer soldiers.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Within a week, more than 12,000 recruits signed up to fight for the Union.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

Indianapolis became a major logistics hub during the war, establishing the city as a crucial military base.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Bod443-52) Between 1860 and 1870, the city's population more than doubled.[[45]](#cite_note-Britannica-45) An estimated 4,000 men from Indianapolis served in 39 regiments, and an estimated 700 died during the war.[[53]](#cite_note-53) On May 20, 1863, Union soldiers attempted to disrupt a statewide Democratic convention at Indianapolis, forcing an adjournment of the proceedings, sarcastically referred to as the [Battle of Pogue's Run](/wiki/Battle_of_Pogue%27s_Run "Battle of Pogue's Run").[[54]](#cite_note-54) Fear turned to panic in July 1863, during [Morgan's Raid](/wiki/Morgan%27s_Raid "Morgan's Raid") into southern Indiana, but Confederate forces turned east toward [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), never reaching Indianapolis.[[55]](#cite_note-55) On April 30, 1865, Lincoln's funeral train made a stop at Indianapolis, where an estimated crowd of more than 100,000 people passed the assassinated president's [bier](/wiki/Bier "Bier") at the [Indiana Statehouse](/wiki/Indiana_Statehouse "Indiana Statehouse").[[52]](#cite_note-Bod443-52)[[56]](#cite_note-Dunn237-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway_-_Otis_Lithograph_Co._border_edit.jpg/250px-Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway_-_Otis_Lithograph_Co._border_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway_-_Otis_Lithograph_Co._border_edit.jpg)

A 1909 advertisement for the [Indianapolis Motor Speedway](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway "Indianapolis Motor Speedway")

Following the Civil War and in the wake of the [Second Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Second_Industrial_Revolution "Second Industrial Revolution"), Indianapolis experienced tremendous growth and prosperity. In 1880, Indianapolis was the world's third-largest pork packing city, after [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), and the second-largest railroad center in the U.S. by 1888.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-Built_Environment-58) By 1890, the city's population surpassed 100,000.[[45]](#cite_note-Britannica-45) Some of the city's most notable businesses were founded during this period of growth and innovation, including [L. S. Ayres](/wiki/L._S._Ayres "L. S. Ayres") (1872), [Eli Lilly and Company](/wiki/Eli_Lilly_and_Company "Eli Lilly and Company") (1876), [Madam C. J. Walker Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Madam_C._J._Walker_Manufacturing_Company "Madam C. J. Walker Manufacturing Company") (1910), and [Allison Transmission](/wiki/Allison_Transmission "Allison Transmission") (1915).

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Some of the city's most prominent architectural features and best known historical events date from the turn of the 20th century. The [Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument](/wiki/Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_(Indianapolis) "Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument (Indianapolis)"), dedicated on May 15, 1902, would later become the city's unofficial symbol.[[59]](#cite_note-59) [Ray Harroun](/wiki/Ray_Harroun "Ray Harroun") won the inaugural running of the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500"), held May 30, [1911](/wiki/1911_Indianapolis_500 "1911 Indianapolis 500"), at [Indianapolis Motor Speedway](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway "Indianapolis Motor Speedway"). Indianapolis was one of the hardest hit cities in the [Great Flood of 1913](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1913 "Great Flood of 1913"), resulting in five known deaths[[60]](#cite_note-IndyProfile-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Bell15-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) and the displacement of 7,000 families.[[63]](#cite_note-BB58182-63)

Once home to 60 automakers, Indianapolis rivaled [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") as a center of automobile manufacturing.[[64]](#cite_note-automaker-64) The city was an early focus of [labor organization](/wiki/Labor_organization "Labor organization").[[45]](#cite_note-Britannica-45) The [Indianapolis streetcar strike of 1913](/wiki/Indianapolis_streetcar_strike_of_1913 "Indianapolis streetcar strike of 1913") and subsequent police mutiny and riots led to the creation of the state's earliest labor-protection laws, including a [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage"), regular work weeks, and improved working conditions.[[65]](#cite_note-d1230-65) The [International Typographical Union](/wiki/International_Typographical_Union "International Typographical Union") and [United Mine Workers of America](/wiki/United_Mine_Workers_of_America "United Mine Workers of America") were among several influential labor unions based in the city.[[45]](#cite_note-Britannica-45)

As a stop on the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad"), Indianapolis developed one of the largest African American populations in the [Northern States](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)"), though this demographic prominence was complicated by the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)"), as outmigration patterns shifted and other northern cities became destinations.[[66]](#cite_note-mumford.albany.edu-66) Led by [D. C. Stephenson](/wiki/D._C._Stephenson "D. C. Stephenson"), the [Indiana Klan](/wiki/Indiana_Klan "Indiana Klan") became the most powerful political and social organization in Indianapolis from 1921 through 1928, controlling the City Council and the Board of School Commissioners. At its height, more than 40% of native-born white males in Indianapolis claimed membership in the Klan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Landmark_for_Peace_Memorial.jpg/250px-Landmark_for_Peace_Memorial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Landmark_for_Peace_Memorial.jpg)

*[Landmark for Peace Memorial](/wiki/Landmark_for_Peace_Memorial "Landmark for Peace Memorial")* in Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Park

While campaigning in the city in 1968, [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy") delivered one of the most lauded [speeches](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy%27s_speech_on_the_assassination_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Robert F. Kennedy's speech on the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.") in 20th century American history following the assassination of [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.")[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) As in most U.S. cities during the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"), the city experienced strained race relations, including [1969 riots](/wiki/1969_Indianapolis_riots "1969 Indianapolis riots"). A 1971 federal court decision forcing [Indianapolis Public Schools](/wiki/Indianapolis_Public_Schools "Indianapolis Public Schools") to implement [desegregation busing](/wiki/Desegregation_busing "Desegregation busing") proved controversial.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

During the mayoral administration of [Richard Lugar](/wiki/Richard_Lugar "Richard Lugar") (1968–1976), the city and county governments consolidated. Known as [Unigov](/wiki/Unigov "Unigov") (a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "unified" and "government"), the [city-county consolidation](/wiki/City-county_consolidation "City-county consolidation") removed bureaucratic redundancies, captured increasingly [suburbanizing](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization") tax revenue, and created a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") [political machine](/wiki/Political_machine "Political machine") that dominated local politics until the early 2000s.[[71]](#cite_note-politics-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) Effective January 1, 1970, Unigov expanded the city's land area by more than 300 square miles (780 km2) and increased its population by some 250,000 people.[[73]](#cite_note-73) It was the first major city-county consolidation to occur in the U.S. without a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") since the creation of the [City of Greater New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York") in 1898.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Lugar is credited with initiating downtown revitalization efforts, overseeing the building of [Market Square Arena](/wiki/Market_Square_Arena "Market Square Arena"), renovations to [Indianapolis City Market](/wiki/Indianapolis_City_Market "Indianapolis City Market"), and the formation of [Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis](/wiki/Indiana_University%E2%80%93Purdue_University_Indianapolis "Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis").[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Amid the changes in government and growth, the city pursued an aggressive economic development strategy to raise the city's stature as a [sports tourism](/wiki/Sports_tourism "Sports tourism") destination, known as the Indianapolis Project.[[76]](#cite_note-76) During the administration of the city's longest-serving mayor, [William Hudnut](/wiki/William_Hudnut "William Hudnut") (1976–1992), millions of dollars were invested into sports venues and public relations campaigns. The strategy was successful in landing the [U.S. Olympic Festival](/wiki/U.S._Olympic_Festival "U.S. Olympic Festival") in 1982, securing the [relocation of the Baltimore Colts](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts_relocation_to_Indianapolis "Baltimore Colts relocation to Indianapolis") in 1984, and hosting the [1987 Pan American Games](/wiki/1987_Pan_American_Games "1987 Pan American Games").[[23]](#cite_note-Naptown-23)

Beginning in 1992, the mayoral administration of [Stephen Goldsmith](/wiki/Stephen_Goldsmith "Stephen Goldsmith") introduced a number of [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") measures to address budget shortfalls through [privatization](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization") and greater reliance on [public–private partnerships](/wiki/Public%E2%80%93private_partnership "Public–private partnership"). Major downtown revitalization projects continued through the 1990s, including the openings of [Circle Centre Mall](/wiki/Circle_Centre_Mall "Circle Centre Mall"), [Victory Field](/wiki/Victory_Field "Victory Field"), and [Gainbridge Fieldhouse](/wiki/Gainbridge_Fieldhouse "Gainbridge Fieldhouse"), as well as ongoing redevelopment of the [Canal and White River State Park](/wiki/Canal_and_White_River_State_Park "Canal and White River State Park") area.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Indy_Central_Canal.jpg/250px-Indy_Central_Canal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indy_Central_Canal.jpg)

The skyline of [Downtown Indianapolis](/wiki/Downtown_Indianapolis "Downtown Indianapolis") seen from [Canal and White River State Park](/wiki/Canal_and_White_River_State_Park "Canal and White River State Park") in 2008

[Bart Peterson](/wiki/Bart_Peterson "Bart Peterson") took office in 2000, the first Democrat elected to the post since [John J. Barton](/wiki/John_J._Barton "John J. Barton")'s [1963 election](/wiki/1963_Indianapolis_mayoral_election "1963 Indianapolis mayoral election").[[78]](#cite_note-78) The Peterson administration focused on [education reform](/wiki/Education_reform "Education reform") and promoting the arts.

[![Panoramic view of Downtown Indianapolis in 1914](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/IN_Indianapolis_1914a.jpg/1280px-IN_Indianapolis_1914a.jpg)](/wiki/File:IN_Indianapolis_1914a.jpg "Downtown Indianapolis in 1914. At left, the Indiana Statehouse. At center-left are the Claypool Hotel (foreground) and Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument (background). The intersection of Illinois St. and the National Road is centered near the bottom of the image. The Indianapolis News Building and Hotel Washington are to the left of Washington Street while the Merchants National Bank Building is visible to the right. At center-right, the Wholesale District. At far-right, the Hotel Severin and Indianapolis Union Station clock tower.")

[Downtown Indianapolis](/wiki/Downtown_Indianapolis "Downtown Indianapolis") in 1914. At left, the [Indiana Statehouse](/wiki/Indiana_Statehouse "Indiana Statehouse"). At center-left are the Claypool Hotel (foreground) and [Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument](/wiki/Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_(Indianapolis) "Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument (Indianapolis)") (background). The intersection of Illinois St. and the [National Road](/wiki/Washington_Street_(Indianapolis) "Washington Street (Indianapolis)") is centered near the bottom of the image. The [Indianapolis News Building](/wiki/Indianapolis_News_Building "Indianapolis News Building") and [Hotel Washington](/wiki/Hotel_Washington_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "Hotel Washington (Indianapolis, Indiana)") are to the left of Washington Street while the [Merchants National Bank Building](/wiki/Barnes_and_Thornburg_Building "Barnes and Thornburg Building") is visible to the right. At center-right, the [Wholesale District](/wiki/Wholesale_District,_Indianapolis "Wholesale District, Indianapolis"). At far-right, the [Hotel Severin](/wiki/Omni_Severin_Hotel "Omni Severin Hotel") and [Indianapolis Union Station](/wiki/Indianapolis_Union_Station "Indianapolis Union Station") clock tower.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2001, the mayor's office became the first in the U.S. to authorize [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools_in_the_United_States "Charter schools in the United States"). [Indianapolis Cultural Districts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Cultural_Districts "Indianapolis Cultural Districts") were designated in 2003, followed by the groundbreaking of the [Indianapolis Cultural Trail](/wiki/Indianapolis_Cultural_Trail "Indianapolis Cultural Trail") in 2007. Further consolidation of city and county units of government resulted in the establishment of the [Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department](/wiki/Indianapolis_Metropolitan_Police_Department "Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department") in 2007. Later that year, [Greg Ballard](/wiki/Greg_Ballard "Greg Ballard") succeeded Peterson in a political upset.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

The Ballard administration oversaw the lease of the city's parking meters and the sale of the city's water and wastewater utilities with proceeds financing street repairs. Ballard pursued several environmental sustainability efforts, including establishing an office of sustainability, installing 200 miles (320 km) of bike lanes and trails, and spearheading a controversial deal to start an electric [carsharing](/wiki/Carsharing "Carsharing") program.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) Two of the city's largest capital projects, the [Indianapolis International Airport](/wiki/Indianapolis_International_Airport "Indianapolis International Airport")'s new terminal and [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium"), were completed in 2008.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) In 2012, construction began on a $2 billion tunnel system, a project known as [DigIndy](/wiki/DigIndy "DigIndy"), which was designed to reduce sewage overflows into the city's waterways.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Since 2016, the administration of [Joe Hogsett](/wiki/Joe_Hogsett "Joe Hogsett") has focused on addressing a rise in gun violence and the city's racial disparities. In recent years, significant capital and operational investments have been made in public safety, criminal justice, and public transit. The city also established rental assistance and [food security](/wiki/Food_security "Food security") programs. In 2020, the [George Floyd protests in Indiana](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests_in_Indiana "George Floyd protests in Indiana") prompted a series of local [police reforms](/wiki/Police_reform_in_the_United_States "Police reform in the United States") and renewed efforts to bolster social services for mental health treatment and homelessness.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) In 2021, [a mass shooting](/wiki/Indianapolis_FedEx_shooting "Indianapolis FedEx shooting") occurred at a [FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx") facility on the city's southwest side, killing nine (including the gunman) and injuring seven others.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Indianapolis_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-09-19.jpg/250px-Indianapolis_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-09-19.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-09-19.jpg)

A 2020 [Sentinel-2](/wiki/Sentinel-2 "Sentinel-2") true-color image of the [Indianapolis metropolitan area](/wiki/Indianapolis_metropolitan_area "Indianapolis metropolitan area")

Indianapolis is located in the [East North Central](/wiki/East_North_Central_states "East North Central states") region of the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), about fourteen miles (23 km) south-southeast of Indiana's [geographic center](/wiki/Geographic_center "Geographic center").[[89]](#cite_note-89) It is situated 98 miles (158 km) northwest of [Cincinnati, Ohio](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"); 107 miles (172 km) north of [Louisville, Kentucky](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"); 164 miles (264 km) southeast of [Chicago, Illinois](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"); and 168 miles (270 km) west of [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio").[[90]](#cite_note-90) According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau"), the [Indianapolis (balance)](/wiki/Indianapolis_(balance) "Indianapolis (balance)") encompasses a total area of 367.9 square miles (953 km2), of which 361.6 square miles (937 km2) is land and 6.3 square miles (16 km2) is water.[[9]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2020-9) It is the [18th-most extensive](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_area "List of United States cities by area") city by land area in the U.S.

As a [consolidated city-county](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county"), Indianapolis's [city limits](/wiki/City_limits "City limits") are coterminous with [Marion County](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana"), except the autonomous and semi-autonomous municipalities outlined in [Unigov](/wiki/Unigov "Unigov").[[45]](#cite_note-Britannica-45)[[91]](#cite_note-StatsIndiana12-91) Nine [civil townships](/wiki/Civil_township "Civil township") form the broadest geographic divisions within the city and county; these are [Center](/wiki/Center_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Center Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Decatur](/wiki/Decatur_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Decatur Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Franklin](/wiki/Franklin_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Franklin Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Lawrence Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Perry](/wiki/Perry_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Perry Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Pike](/wiki/Pike_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Pike Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Warren](/wiki/Warren_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Warren Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Washington Township, Marion County, Indiana"), and [Wayne](/wiki/Wayne_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Wayne Township, Marion County, Indiana") townships.[[92]](#cite_note-Townships-92) The consolidated city-county borders the adjacent counties of [Boone](/wiki/Boone_County,_Indiana "Boone County, Indiana") to the northwest; [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton_County,_Indiana "Hamilton County, Indiana") to the north; [Hancock](/wiki/Hancock_County,_Indiana "Hancock County, Indiana") to the east; [Shelby](/wiki/Shelby_County,_Indiana "Shelby County, Indiana") to the southeast; [Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_County,_Indiana "Johnson County, Indiana") to the south; [Morgan](/wiki/Morgan_County,_Indiana "Morgan County, Indiana") to the southwest; and [Hendricks](/wiki/Hendricks_County,_Indiana "Hendricks County, Indiana") to the west.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

Between 2.6 million and 11,700 years ago, the Indianapolis area was situated on the southern margin of the [Laurentide Ice Sheet](/wiki/Laurentide_Ice_Sheet "Laurentide Ice Sheet"). The erosive advance and retreat of glacial ice produced a flat or gently sloping landscape, known as a [till plain](/wiki/Till_plain "Till plain"). Elevations across Indianapolis vary from about 650 feet (198 m) to 900 feet (274 m) [above mean sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").[[94]](#cite_note-Geology-94) Indianapolis is located in the [West Fork White River](/wiki/White_River_(Indiana)#West_Fork "White River (Indiana)") [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin"), part of the larger [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") watershed via the [Wabash](/wiki/Wabash_River "Wabash River") and [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") rivers.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The White River flows 31 miles (50 km) north-to-south through the city and is fed by some 35 streams, including Eagle Creek, [Fall Creek](/wiki/Fall_Creek_(Indiana) "Fall Creek (Indiana)"), Pleasant Run, and [Pogue's Run](/wiki/Pogue%27s_Run "Pogue's Run").[[96]](#cite_note-White_River-96) The city's largest waterbodies are artificial [quarry lakes](/wiki/Quarry_lake "Quarry lake") and [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir").

### Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Indianapolis_skyline_2019.jpg/1280px-Indianapolis_skyline_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_skyline_2019.jpg "Downtown Indianapolis skyline viewed from the southwest, 2019")

[Downtown Indianapolis](/wiki/Downtown_Indianapolis "Downtown Indianapolis") skyline viewed from the southwest, 2019

Indianapolis is an example of a [planned city](/wiki/Planned_city "Planned city"). In 1821, the [Indiana General Assembly](/wiki/Indiana_General_Assembly "Indiana General Assembly") selected a site near the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of the [White River](/wiki/White_River_(Indiana) "White River (Indiana)") and [Fall Creek](/wiki/Fall_Creek_(Indiana) "Fall Creek (Indiana)") to serve as the new state capital, adopting a plan co-designed by surveyors [Alexander Ralston](/wiki/Alexander_Ralston "Alexander Ralston") and [Elias Pym Fordham](/wiki/Elias_Pym_Fordham "Elias Pym Fordham").[[97]](#cite_note-97) The [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan") called for a town of 1 square mile (2.6 km2) centered on a [traffic circle](/wiki/Traffic_circle "Traffic circle") (from which Indianapolis's "Circle City" nickname originates).[[98]](#cite_note-98) Four diagonal avenues—[Indiana](/wiki/Indiana_Avenue "Indiana Avenue") (northwest), Kentucky (southwest), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts_Avenue,_Indianapolis "Massachusetts Avenue, Indianapolis") (northeast), and Virginia (southeast)—radiated a block from the circle.[[99]](#cite_note-99) The city's address numbering system originates at the intersection of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Street_(Indianapolis) "Washington Street (Indianapolis)") (running east–west) and [Meridian](/wiki/Meridian_Street_(Indianapolis) "Meridian Street (Indianapolis)") streets (running north–south).[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In the 2020 census, Indianapolis was among the 20 largest cities in the U.S. by both population and land area. The city’s population density of 2,455 people per square mile (948/km2) [ranked 222nd](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") among major U.S. cities. This contrast is evident in Indianapolis's cityscape where [low-density development](/wiki/Low-density_development "Low-density development") patterns dominate and some 14,600 acres (5,900 ha) of farmland remain within its municipal boundaries.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

#### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

See also: [List of neighborhoods in Indianapolis](/wiki/List_of_neighborhoods_in_Indianapolis "List of neighborhoods in Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Webster_Avenue_in_Irvington_Terrace.jpg/250px-Webster_Avenue_in_Irvington_Terrace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Webster_Avenue_in_Irvington_Terrace.jpg)

Single-family homes in the [Irvington Terrace Historic District](/wiki/Irvington_Terrace_Historic_District "Irvington Terrace Historic District")

For statistical purposes, the consolidated city-county is organized into 99 "neighborhood areas" with most containing numerous individual historic and cultural districts, subdivisions, and some semi-autonomous towns. In total, some 500 self-identified neighborhood associations are listed in the city's Registered Community Organization system.[[102]](#cite_note-102) As a result of the city's expansive land area, Indianapolis has a unique [urban-to-rural transect](/wiki/Transect_(urban) "Transect (urban)"), ranging from dense urban neighborhoods to suburban [tract housing](/wiki/Tract_housing "Tract housing") subdivisions, to rural villages.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

Typical of American cities in the Midwest, Indianapolis urbanized in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, resulting in the development of relatively dense, well-defined neighborhoods clustered around streetcar corridors, especially in [Center Township](/wiki/Center_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Center Township, Marion County, Indiana").[[104]](#cite_note-104) Notable [streetcar suburbs](/wiki/Streetcar_suburb "Streetcar suburb") include [Broad Ripple](/wiki/Broad_Ripple_Village,_Indianapolis "Broad Ripple Village, Indianapolis"), [Irvington](/wiki/Irvington_Historic_District_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "Irvington Historic District (Indianapolis, Indiana)"), and [University Heights](/wiki/University_Heights,_Indianapolis "University Heights, Indianapolis").[[105]](#cite_note-Neighborhoods_and_Communities-105) Starting in the mid-20th century, the [post–World War II economic expansion](/wiki/Post%E2%80%93World_War_II_economic_expansion "Post–World War II economic expansion") and subsequent [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization") greatly influenced the city's development patterns. From 1950 to 1970, nearly 100,000 housing units were built in Marion County, most outside Center Township in suburban neighborhoods such as [Castleton](/wiki/Castleton,_Indianapolis "Castleton, Indianapolis"), [Eagledale](/wiki/Eagledale,_Indianapolis "Eagledale, Indianapolis"), and [Nora](/wiki/Nora,_Indianapolis "Nora, Indianapolis").[[105]](#cite_note-Neighborhoods_and_Communities-105)

Since the 2000s, [downtown Indianapolis](/wiki/Downtown_Indianapolis "Downtown Indianapolis") and surrounding neighborhoods have seen increased reinvestment mirroring nationwide market trends, driven by [empty nesters](/wiki/Empty_nesters "Empty nesters") and [millennials](/wiki/Millennials "Millennials").[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107) Renewed interest in urban living has been met with some dispute regarding [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") and [affordable housing](/wiki/Affordable_housing "Affordable housing").[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) According to a Center for Community Progress report, neighborhoods like [Cottage Home](/wiki/Cottage_Home_Historic_District "Cottage Home Historic District") and [Fall Creek Place](/wiki/Fall_Creek_Place,_Indianapolis "Fall Creek Place, Indianapolis") have experienced measurable gentrification since 2000.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The [North Meridian Street Historic District](/wiki/North_Meridian_Street_Historic_District "North Meridian Street Historic District") is among the most affluent urban neighborhoods in the U.S., with a mean [household income](/wiki/Household_income "Household income") of $102,599 in 2017.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

#### Historic districts and landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Historic districts and landmarks")]

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Indianapolis](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Indianapolis "List of tallest buildings in Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Indiana_Central_Canal_-_2013_June_-_01.jpg/250px-Indiana_Central_Canal_-_2013_June_-_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indiana_Central_Canal_-_2013_June_-_01.jpg)

Segment of the former Indiana Central Canal, an American Water Landmark

The 8-mile-long (13 km) [Indiana Central Canal](/wiki/Indiana_Central_Canal "Indiana Central Canal") is the oldest extant artificial facility in the city, dating to the 1830s. Between 1985 and 2001, nearly 1.5 miles (2.4 km) of the former canal in downtown Indianapolis were redeveloped into a cultural and recreational amenity. North of 18th Street, the canal retains much of its original appearance, flowing through the north side neighborhoods of [Riverside](/wiki/Riverside,_Indianapolis "Riverside, Indianapolis"), [Butler–Tarkington](/wiki/Butler%E2%80%93Tarkington,_Indianapolis "Butler–Tarkington, Indianapolis"), [Rocky Ripple](/wiki/Rocky_Ripple,_Indiana "Rocky Ripple, Indiana"), and [Broad Ripple](/wiki/Broad_Ripple_Village,_Indianapolis "Broad Ripple Village, Indianapolis"). This segment has been recognized as an [American Water Landmark](/wiki/American_Water_Landmark "American Water Landmark") since 1971.[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114)

More than 260 properties and historic districts are listed on the [National Register of Historic Places in Marion County](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Marion_County,_Indiana "National Register of Historic Places listings in Marion County, Indiana"); most sites are located in Center Township, [listed separately](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Center_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "National Register of Historic Places listings in Center Township, Marion County, Indiana"). This includes nine [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"): the [Athenæum (Das Deutsche Haus)](/wiki/Athen%C3%A6um_(Das_Deutsche_Haus) "Athenæum (Das Deutsche Haus)"), the [Benjamin Harrison House](/wiki/Benjamin_Harrison_House "Benjamin Harrison House"), the [Broad Ripple Park Carousel](/wiki/Broad_Ripple_Park_Carousel "Broad Ripple Park Carousel"), [Hinkle Fieldhouse](/wiki/Hinkle_Fieldhouse "Hinkle Fieldhouse"), the [Indiana World War Memorial](/wiki/Indiana_World_War_Memorial "Indiana World War Memorial"), the [Indianapolis Motor Speedway](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway "Indianapolis Motor Speedway"), the [James Whitcomb Riley House](/wiki/James_Whitcomb_Riley_Museum_Home "James Whitcomb Riley Museum Home"), the [Madam C. J. Walker Building](/wiki/Madam_Walker_Legacy_Center "Madam Walker Legacy Center"), and [Oldfields–Lilly House & Gardens](/wiki/Oldfields "Oldfields").

The [Indianapolis Historic Preservation Commission](/wiki/Indianapolis_Historic_Preservation_Commission "Indianapolis Historic Preservation Commission") oversees 13 local historic districts and five conservation areas.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Notable districts include [Chatham–Arch](/wiki/Chatham%E2%80%93Arch,_Indianapolis "Chatham–Arch, Indianapolis") and [Massachusetts Avenue](/wiki/Mass_Ave_Cultural_Arts_District "Mass Ave Cultural Arts District"), [Cottage Home](/wiki/Cottage_Home_Historic_District "Cottage Home Historic District"), [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland_Historic_District "Cumberland Historic District"), [Fletcher Place](/wiki/Fletcher_Place "Fletcher Place"), [Fountain Square](/wiki/Fountain_Square,_Indianapolis "Fountain Square, Indianapolis"), [Herron–Morton Place](/wiki/Herron%E2%80%93Morton_Place_Historic_District "Herron–Morton Place Historic District"), [Irvington](/wiki/Irvington_Historic_District_(Indianapolis) "Irvington Historic District (Indianapolis)"), [Lockefield Gardens](/wiki/Lockefield_Gardens "Lockefield Gardens"), [Lockerbie Square](/wiki/Lockerbie_Square_Historic_District "Lockerbie Square Historic District"), [Monument Circle](/wiki/Monument_Circle "Monument Circle"), [New Augusta](/wiki/New_Augusta_Historic_District "New Augusta Historic District"), the [Old Northside](/wiki/Old_Northside_Historic_District "Old Northside Historic District"), [Ransom Place](/wiki/Ransom_Place_Historic_District "Ransom Place Historic District"), [St. Joseph](/wiki/St._Joseph_Neighborhood_Historic_District "St. Joseph Neighborhood Historic District"), the [Wholesale District](/wiki/Wholesale_District,_Indianapolis "Wholesale District, Indianapolis"), and [Woodruff Place](/wiki/Woodruff_Place,_Indianapolis "Woodruff Place, Indianapolis").[[116]](#cite_note-116)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_-_February_2019_-_INvets_05.jpg/250px-Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_-_February_2019_-_INvets_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_-_February_2019_-_INvets_05.jpg)

The [Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument](/wiki/Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_(Indianapolis) "Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument (Indianapolis)") (completed 1902) stands in contrast to [Salesforce Tower](/wiki/Salesforce_Tower_(Indianapolis) "Salesforce Tower (Indianapolis)") (completed 1990), reflecting different periods in the development of Indianapolis’s skyline.

Following the dedication of the 284-foot (87 m) [Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument](/wiki/Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_(Indianapolis) "Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument (Indianapolis)") in 1902, Indianapolis enacted its first [height restriction laws](/wiki/Height_restriction_laws "Height restriction laws") to protect views of the monument.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1962, the 372-foot (113 m) [City-County Building](/wiki/City-County_Building_(Indianapolis) "City-County Building (Indianapolis)") became the first high-rise in the city to surpass the monument’s height.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The tallest residential buildings in Indianapolis, the twin 295-foot (90 m) 30-floor [Riley Towers](/wiki/Riley_Towers "Riley Towers"), were completed in 1963.[[117]](#cite_note-117) The 48-floor [Salesforce Tower](/wiki/Salesforce_Tower_(Indianapolis) "Salesforce Tower (Indianapolis)"), completed in 1990, is the city’s tallest, with a roof height of 701 feet (214 m). Its distinctive twin antenna masts bring the building’s height to 811 feet (247 m).[[118]](#cite_note-118)

[Indiana limestone](/wiki/Indiana_limestone "Indiana limestone") has been a signature building material in Indianapolis since the 1800s, featured prominently in the city’s monuments, churches, commercial, and civic buildings.[[119]](#cite_note-119) Examples include [Roberts Park Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/Roberts_Park_Methodist_Episcopal_Church "Roberts Park Methodist Episcopal Church") (1876), the [Indiana Statehouse](/wiki/Indiana_Statehouse "Indiana Statehouse") (1888), the [Majestic Building](/wiki/Majestic_Building_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "Majestic Building (Indianapolis, Indiana)") (1896), the Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument (1902), [Birch Bayh Federal Building and United States Courthouse](/wiki/Birch_Bayh_Federal_Building_and_United_States_Courthouse "Birch Bayh Federal Building and United States Courthouse") (1905), [Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral](/wiki/Saints_Peter_and_Paul_Cathedral_(Indianapolis) "Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral (Indianapolis)") (1907), the [Indianapolis Masonic Temple](/wiki/Indianapolis_Masonic_Temple "Indianapolis Masonic Temple") (1909), [Old Indianapolis City Hall](/wiki/Old_Indianapolis_City_Hall "Old Indianapolis City Hall") (1910), [Central Library](/wiki/Central_Library_(Indianapolis) "Central Library (Indianapolis)") (1917), the [Scottish Rite Cathedral](/wiki/Scottish_Rite_Cathedral_(Indianapolis) "Scottish Rite Cathedral (Indianapolis)") (1927), [Circle Tower](/wiki/Circle_Tower "Circle Tower") (1929), the Indiana World War Memorial (1933), the [Indiana State Library and Historical Bureau](/wiki/Indiana_State_Library_and_Historical_Bureau "Indiana State Library and Historical Bureau") (1934), [Clowes Memorial Hall](/wiki/Clowes_Memorial_Hall "Clowes Memorial Hall") (1963), and [OneAmerica Tower](/wiki/OneAmerica_Tower "OneAmerica Tower") (1982).

#### Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Parks")]

See also: [List of parks in Indianapolis](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_Indianapolis "List of parks in Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Eagle_Creek_Park_%2825135p%29.jpg/250px-Eagle_Creek_Park_%2825135p%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eagle_Creek_Park_(25135p).jpg)

[Eagle Creek Park](/wiki/Eagle_Creek_Park "Eagle Creek Park") is the largest and most visited park in Indianapolis.

The city of Indianapolis maintains 212 public parks, totaling 11,258 acres (4,556 ha) or about 5.1% of the city's land area.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) [Garfield Park](/wiki/Garfield_Park_(Indianapolis) "Garfield Park (Indianapolis)"), the city's first municipal park, opened in 1876 as Southern Park.[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) [George Kessler](/wiki/George_Kessler "George Kessler")'s [Indianapolis Park and Boulevard Plan](/wiki/Indianapolis_Park_and_Boulevard_System "Indianapolis Park and Boulevard System") (1909) linked notable parks, such as [Brookside](/wiki/Brookside_Park_(Indianapolis) "Brookside Park (Indianapolis)"), [Ellenberger](/wiki/Irvington_Historic_District_(Indianapolis,_Indiana)#Parks "Irvington Historic District (Indianapolis, Indiana)"), Garfield, and [Riverside](/wiki/Riverside_Park_(Indianapolis) "Riverside Park (Indianapolis)"), with a system of parkways following the city's waterways.[[124]](#cite_note-124) The system's 3,474 acres (1,406 ha) were added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2003.[[125]](#cite_note-125) [Eagle Creek Park](/wiki/Eagle_Creek_Park "Eagle Creek Park"), Indianapolis's largest and most visited park, ranks among the [largest municipal parks](/wiki/List_of_urban_parks_by_size "List of urban parks by size") in the U.S., covering 4,766 acres (1,929 ha).[[126]](#cite_note-126)

Marion County is also home to parks managed by the State of Indiana, including [Fort Harrison State Park](/wiki/Fort_Harrison_State_Park "Fort Harrison State Park") and [White River State Park](/wiki/White_River_State_Park "White River State Park"). Established in 1996, Fort Harrison State Park covers 1,744 acres (706 ha) that are overseen by the [Indiana Department of Natural Resources](/wiki/Indiana_Department_of_Natural_Resources "Indiana Department of Natural Resources").[[127]](#cite_note-127) Since 1979, White River has been owned and operated by the White River State Park Development Commission, a quasi-governmental agency.[[128]](#cite_note-128) White River's 250 acres (100 ha) are home to several attractions, including the [Indianapolis Zoo](/wiki/Indianapolis_Zoo "Indianapolis Zoo") and [White River Gardens](/wiki/White_River_Gardens "White River Gardens"). Two [land trusts](/wiki/Land_trust "Land trust") are active in the city managing several sites for [nature conservation](/wiki/Nature_conservation "Nature conservation") throughout the region.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130)

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Reminds_me_of_the_bridgestone_commercial_%282669256227%29.jpg/250px-Reminds_me_of_the_bridgestone_commercial_%282669256227%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reminds_me_of_the_bridgestone_commercial_(2669256227).jpg)

A [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer") among [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") trees in Indianapolis

Indianapolis is situated in the [Southern Great Lakes forests](/wiki/Southern_Great_Lakes_forests "Southern Great Lakes forests") [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") which in turn is located within the larger [temperate broadleaf and mixed forests](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forests "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forests") [biome](/wiki/Biome "Biome"), as defined by the [World Wide Fund for Nature](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature").[[131]](#cite_note-ecoregion-131) Based on the [U.S. Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/U.S._Environmental_Protection_Agency "U.S. Environmental Protection Agency")'s [alternative classification system](/wiki/List_of_ecoregions_in_Indiana "List of ecoregions in Indiana"), the city is located in the [Eastern Corn Belt Plains](/wiki/Corn_Belt "Corn Belt"), an area of the country known for its fertile soil.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Much of the deciduous forests that once covered 98% of the region were [cleared](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") for agriculture and [urban development](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"), contributing to considerable [habitat loss](/wiki/Habitat_destruction "Habitat destruction").[[131]](#cite_note-ecoregion-131)[[133]](#cite_note-Holland,_et_al.-133) Indianapolis's current urban tree canopy averages approximately 33%.[[134]](#cite_note-134) A rare example of [old-growth forest](/wiki/Old-growth_forest "Old-growth forest") in the city can be found on 15 acres (6.1 ha) of [Crown Hill Cemetery](/wiki/Crown_Hill_Cemetery "Crown Hill Cemetery")'s North Woods in the [Butler–Tarkington](/wiki/Butler%E2%80%93Tarkington,_Indianapolis "Butler–Tarkington, Indianapolis") neighborhood.[[135]](#cite_note-135) The cemetery's 555 acres (225 ha) represents the largest green space in [Center Township](/wiki/Center_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Center Township, Marion County, Indiana"), home to an abundance of wildlife and some 130 species of trees.[[136]](#cite_note-136) Native trees most common to the area include varieties of [ash](/wiki/Fraxinus "Fraxinus"), [maple](/wiki/Maple "Maple"), and [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak").[[131]](#cite_note-ecoregion-131) Several [invasive species](/wiki/Invasive_species "Invasive species") are also common in Indianapolis, including [tree of heaven](/wiki/Ailanthus_altissima "Ailanthus altissima"), [wintercreeper](/wiki/Euonymus_fortunei "Euonymus fortunei"), [Amur honeysuckle](/wiki/Lonicera_maackii "Lonicera maackii"), and [Callery pear](/wiki/Pyrus_calleryana "Pyrus calleryana").[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

A 2016 [bioblitz](/wiki/Bioblitz "Bioblitz") along three of the city's [riparian corridors](/wiki/Riparian_corridor "Riparian corridor") found 590 [taxa](/wiki/Taxon "Taxon").[[133]](#cite_note-Holland,_et_al.-133) [Urban wildlife](/wiki/Urban_wildlife "Urban wildlife") common to the Indianapolis area include mammals such as the [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), [eastern chipmunk](/wiki/Eastern_chipmunk "Eastern chipmunk"), [eastern cottontail](/wiki/Eastern_cottontail "Eastern cottontail"), and the [eastern grey](/wiki/Eastern_gray_squirrel "Eastern gray squirrel") and [American red squirrels](/wiki/American_red_squirrel "American red squirrel").[[131]](#cite_note-ecoregion-131) In recent years, local [raccoon](/wiki/Raccoon "Raccoon") and [groundhog](/wiki/Groundhog "Groundhog") populations have increased alongside sightings of [American badgers](/wiki/American_badger "American badger"), [beavers](/wiki/North_American_beaver "North American beaver"), [mink](/wiki/American_mink "American mink"), [coyotes](/wiki/Coyote "Coyote"), and [red fox](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox").[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) Birds native to the area include the [northern cardinal](/wiki/Northern_cardinal "Northern cardinal"), [wood thrush](/wiki/Wood_thrush "Wood thrush"), [eastern screech owl](/wiki/Eastern_screech_owl "Eastern screech owl"), [mourning dove](/wiki/Mourning_dove "Mourning dove"), [pileated](/wiki/Pileated_woodpecker "Pileated woodpecker") and [red-bellied woodpeckers](/wiki/Red-bellied_woodpecker "Red-bellied woodpecker"), and [wild turkey](/wiki/Wild_turkey "Wild turkey").[[131]](#cite_note-ecoregion-131) Located in the [Mississippi Flyway](/wiki/Mississippi_Flyway "Mississippi Flyway"), the city sees more than 400 migratory bird species throughout the year.[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143) Some 57 species of fish can be found in the city's waterways, including [bass](/wiki/Bass_(fish) "Bass (fish)") and [sunfish](/wiki/Centrarchidae "Centrarchidae").[[96]](#cite_note-White_River-96) Some federally-designated [endangered](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species") and [threatened species](/wiki/Threatened_species "Threatened species") are native to the Indianapolis area, including several species of freshwater [mussels](/wiki/Mussel "Mussel"), the [rusty patched bumble bee](/wiki/Bombus_affinis "Bombus affinis"), [Indiana bat](/wiki/Indiana_bat "Indiana bat"), [northern long-eared bat](/wiki/Myotis_septentrionalis "Myotis septentrionalis"), and the [running buffalo clover](/wiki/Trifolium_stoloniferum "Trifolium stoloniferum").[[144]](#cite_note-144)

In recent years, the [National Wildlife Federation](/wiki/National_Wildlife_Federation "National Wildlife Federation") has ranked Indianapolis among the ten most wildlife-friendly cities in the U.S.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/ButlerFall2014_08.JPG/250px-ButlerFall2014_08.JPG)](/wiki/File:ButlerFall2014_08.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Butler_Winter_2015_04.jpg/250px-Butler_Winter_2015_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Butler_Winter_2015_04.jpg)

[Fall foliage](/wiki/Autumn_leaf_color "Autumn leaf color") and a late-winter snowfall on the [Butler University](/wiki/Butler_University "Butler University") campus

Indianapolis has a [hot-summer humid continental climate](/wiki/Hot-summer_humid_continental_climate "Hot-summer humid continental climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Dfa*), but can be considered a borderline [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfa*) using the −3 °C (27 °F) isotherm. It experiences four distinct seasons.[[146]](#cite_note-146) The city lies at the transition between [USDA](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") plant [hardiness zones](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 6a and 6b.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

Typically, summers are fairly hot, humid, and wet. Winters are generally cold with moderate snowfall. The July daily average temperature is 75.4 °F (24.1 °C). High temperatures reach or exceed 90 °F (32 °C) an average of 18 days each year,[[148]](#cite_note-NOAA_Indianapolis_Weatherbox-148) and occasionally exceed 95 °F (35 °C). Spring and autumn are usually pleasant, if at times unpredictable. Midday temperature drops exceeding 30 °F or 17 °C are common during March and April, and instances of very warm days (80 °F or 27 °C) followed within 36 hours by snowfall are not unusual during these months. Winters are cold, with an average January temperature of 28.1 °F (−2.2 °C). Temperatures dip to 0 °F (−18 °C) or below an average of 3.7 nights per year.[[148]](#cite_note-NOAA_Indianapolis_Weatherbox-148)

The rainiest months occur in the spring and summer, with slightly higher averages during May, June, and July. May is typically the wettest, with an average of 5.05 inches (12.8 cm) of rain.[[148]](#cite_note-NOAA_Indianapolis_Weatherbox-148) Most rain is derived from thunderstorm activity. There is no distinct dry season, although occasional droughts occur. Severe weather is not uncommon, particularly in the spring and summer months. Indianapolis experiences an average of 20 thunderstorm days annually.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

Indianapolis's average annual rainfall is 42.4 inches (108 cm). Snowfall averages 25.9 inches (66 cm) per season. Official temperature extremes range from 106 °F (41 °C), set on [July 14, 1936](/wiki/1936_North_American_heat_wave "1936 North American heat wave"),[[150]](#cite_note-IndyClimate-150) to −27 °F (−33 °C), set on [January 19, 1994](/wiki/1994_North_American_cold_wave "1994 North American cold wave").[[150]](#cite_note-IndyClimate-150)[[151]](#cite_note-TWC-151)

The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") considers Indianapolis as two entities: the consolidated city and [the city's remainder, or balance](/wiki/Indianapolis_(balance) "Indianapolis (balance)"). The consolidated city is coterminous with [Marion County](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana"), except the independent municipalities of [Beech Grove](/wiki/Beech_Grove,_Indiana "Beech Grove, Indiana"), [Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence,_Indiana "Lawrence, Indiana"), [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Indiana "Southport, Indiana"), and [Speedway](/wiki/Speedway,_Indiana "Speedway, Indiana").[[157]](#cite_note-2010_pop_hh-159) The city's balance excludes the populations of ten semi-autonomous municipalities that are included in totals for the consolidated city.[[91]](#cite_note-StatsIndiana12-91) These are [Clermont](/wiki/Clermont,_Indiana "Clermont, Indiana"), [Crows Nest](/wiki/Crows_Nest,_Indiana "Crows Nest, Indiana"), [Homecroft](/wiki/Homecroft,_Indiana "Homecroft, Indiana"), [Meridian Hills](/wiki/Meridian_Hills,_Indiana "Meridian Hills, Indiana"), [North Crows Nest](/wiki/North_Crows_Nest,_Indiana "North Crows Nest, Indiana"), [Rocky Ripple](/wiki/Rocky_Ripple,_Indiana "Rocky Ripple, Indiana"), [Spring Hill](/wiki/Spring_Hill,_Indiana "Spring Hill, Indiana"), [Warren Park](/wiki/Warren_Park,_Indiana "Warren Park, Indiana"), [Williams Creek](/wiki/Williams_Creek,_Indiana "Williams Creek, Indiana"), and [Wynnedale](/wiki/Wynnedale,_Indiana "Wynnedale, Indiana").[[157]](#cite_note-2010_pop_hh-159)[[8]](#cite_note-Unigov-8) An eleventh town, [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Indiana "Cumberland, Indiana"), is partially included.[[158]](#cite_note-160)[[159]](#cite_note-161)

In 2015, [Brookings](/wiki/Brookings_Institution "Brookings Institution") characterized the Indianapolis metropolitan area as a minor-emerging immigrant gateway with a foreign-born population of 126,767, or 6.4% of the total population, a 131% increase from 2000.[[160]](#cite_note-162) Much of this growth can be attributed to thousands of Burmese-Chin refugees who have settled in Indianapolis, particularly [Perry Township](/wiki/Perry_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Perry Township, Marion County, Indiana"), since the late 1990s.[[161]](#cite_note-163) Indianapolis is home to one of the largest concentrations of [Chin people](/wiki/Chin_people "Chin people") outside of [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") (formerly Burma), with an estimated population ranging from 17,000 to 24,000.[[162]](#cite_note-164)[[163]](#cite_note-165)[[164]](#cite_note-166)

The [Williams Institute](/wiki/Williams_Institute "Williams Institute") reported that the Indianapolis metropolitan area had an estimated 4.6% [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") adult population in 2020, totaling about 68,000.[[165]](#cite_note-167)

At the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Indianapolis had a population of 887,642 and a population density of 2,455 people per square mile (948/km2). The estimated population was 880,621 in 2022.[[14]](#cite_note-QuickFacts22-14) By population, Indianapolis is the state's largest city and the country's 16th largest.[[172]](#cite_note-174)

The [Indianapolis metropolitan area](/wiki/Indianapolis_metropolitan_area "Indianapolis metropolitan area"), officially the Indianapolis–Carmel–Greenwood, IN metropolitan statistical area (MSA), consists of Marion County and the surrounding counties of [Boone](/wiki/Boone_County,_Indiana "Boone County, Indiana"), [Brown](/wiki/Brown_County,_Indiana "Brown County, Indiana"), [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton_County,_Indiana "Hamilton County, Indiana"), [Hancock](/wiki/Hancock_County,_Indiana "Hancock County, Indiana"), [Hendricks](/wiki/Hendricks_County,_Indiana "Hendricks County, Indiana"), [Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_County,_Indiana "Johnson County, Indiana"), [Madison](/wiki/Madison_County,_Indiana "Madison County, Indiana"), [Morgan](/wiki/Morgan_County,_Indiana "Morgan County, Indiana"), [Shelby](/wiki/Shelby_County,_Indiana "Shelby County, Indiana"), and [Tipton](/wiki/Tipton_County,_Indiana "Tipton County, Indiana").[[173]](#cite_note-OMB_Bulletin_No._23-01-175) In 2020, the metropolitan area's population was 2,111,040, the most populous in Indiana and home to 31% of the state's residents. In 2022, the estimated population was 2,141,779.[[15]](#cite_note-IBRC_MSA-15) In 2020, the larger Indianapolis–Carmel–Muncie, IN combined statistical area (CSA) had a population of 2,492,514, home to nearly 37% of Indiana residents across 20 of Indiana's 92 counties.[[173]](#cite_note-OMB_Bulletin_No._23-01-175) In 2022, the estimated population was 2,524,790.[[16]](#cite_note-IBRC_CSA-16)

In the 2010 census, 97.2% of the Indianapolis population was reported as one race: 61.8% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 27.5% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_American "African American"), 2.1% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American") (0.4% Burmese, 0.4% Indian, 0.3% Chinese, 0.3% Filipino, 0.1% Korean, 0.1% Vietnamese, 0.1% Japanese, 0.1% Thai, 0.1% other Asian); 0.3% [American Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), and 5.5% as other. The remaining 2.8% of the population was reported as [multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial_American "Multiracial American") (two or more races).[[174]](#cite_note-Factfinder2-176) Indianapolis's [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") community was 9.4% of the city's population in the 2010 U.S. Census: 6.9% Mexican, 0.4% Puerto Rican, 0.1% Cuban, and 2% as other.[[174]](#cite_note-Factfinder2-176) Sometime between 2020 and 2023, Marion County became majority non-white.[[175]](#cite_note-nw-177)

In 2010, the median age for Indianapolis was 33.7 years. Age distribution for the city's inhabitants was 25% under the age of 18; 4.4% were between 18 and 21; 16.3% were age 21 to 65; and 13.1% were age 65 or older.[[174]](#cite_note-Factfinder2-176) For every 100 females, there were 93 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 90 males.[[176]](#cite_note-178)

The 2010 census reported 332,199 households in Indianapolis, with an average household size of 2.42 and an average family size of 3.08.[[174]](#cite_note-Factfinder2-176) Of the total households, 59.3% were family households, with 28.2% of these including the family's own children under the age of 18; 36.5% were husband-wife families; 17.2% had a female householder (with no husband present) and 5.6% had a male householder (with no wife present). The remaining 40.7% were non-family households.[[174]](#cite_note-Factfinder2-176) As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit), 32% of the non-family households included individuals living alone, 8.3% of these households included individuals age 65 years of age or older.[[174]](#cite_note-Factfinder2-176)

The U.S. Census Bureau's 2007–2011 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") indicated the median household income for Indianapolis city was $42,704, and the median family income was $53,161.[[177]](#cite_note-AmSurvey-179) Median income for males working full-time, year-round, was $42,101, compared to $34,788 for females. Per capita income for the city was $24,430. 14.7% of families and 18.9% of the city's total population lived below the poverty line. 28.3% of those in poverty were under the age of 18 and 9.2% were age 65 or older.[[177]](#cite_note-AmSurvey-179)

### Homelessness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Homelessness")]

In 2023, a [Point-In-Time Count](/wiki/Point-In-Time_Count "Point-In-Time Count") conducted by the Coalition for Homelessness Intervention and Prevention identified 1,619 homeless individuals in Indianapolis. About 78% of the city's homeless population was sheltered, with 64% living in emergency shelters and 14% in transitional housing. The remaining 22% were unsheltered.[[178]](#cite_note-180)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/St_John%2C_Indianapolis%2C_IN_%2814400587460%29.jpg/250px-St_John%2C_Indianapolis%2C_IN_%2814400587460%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_John,_Indianapolis,_IN_(14400587460).jpg)

Interior of [St. John the Evangelist Catholic Church](/wiki/St._John_the_Evangelist_Catholic_Church_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "St. John the Evangelist Catholic Church (Indianapolis, Indiana)"), a parish of the [Archdiocese of Indianapolis](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Indianapolis "Archdiocese of Indianapolis") and the oldest Catholic congregation in the city.

Religion has played a central role in shaping the city's healthcare, political, and social service systems.[[180]](#cite_note-182)[[181]](#cite_note-Lantzer-183)[[182]](#cite_note-184) [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the prevalent religion in the Indianapolis area. In 2016, the [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute") identified the largest groupings of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States") by faith tradition: 23% [Evangelical Protestant](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), 20% [Mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant"), and 11% [Black Protestant](/wiki/Black_church "Black church"), respectively. At 15%, the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") was the largest single denomination. Other predominant denominations include [Baptists](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") (15%), [Methodists](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") (8%), [Disciples of Christ](/wiki/Disciples_of_Christ "Disciples of Christ") (5%), [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") (5%), [Jehovah’s Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%E2%80%99s_Witnesses "Jehovah’s Witnesses") (3%), and [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") (2%). About 3% of the population adheres to non-Christian religions. Some 22% of residents identified as religiously "unaffiliated", consistent with the national average of 22.7%.[[179]](#cite_note-AVA-181)

Indianapolis is home to roughly 1,200 congregations representing numerous faiths, including some 100 Christian denominations.[[183]](#cite_note-185) Some of the city’s oldest congregations include [Meridian Street United Methodist Church](/wiki/Meridian_Street_United_Methodist_Church "Meridian Street United Methodist Church") (1821),[[184]](#cite_note-186) [Central Christian Church](/wiki/Central_Christian_Church_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "Central Christian Church (Indianapolis, Indiana)") (1833),[[185]](#cite_note-Seay-187) [Bethel A.M.E. Church](/wiki/Bethel_A.M.E._Church_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "Bethel A.M.E. Church (Indianapolis, Indiana)") (1836),[[186]](#cite_note-188) [Christ Church Cathedral](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral_(Indianapolis) "Christ Church Cathedral (Indianapolis)") (1837),[[187]](#cite_note-189) [St. John the Evangelist Catholic Church](/wiki/St._John_the_Evangelist_Catholic_Church_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "St. John the Evangelist Catholic Church (Indianapolis, Indiana)") (1837),[[188]](#cite_note-190) [Second Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Second_Presbyterian_Church_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "Second Presbyterian Church (Indianapolis, Indiana)") (1838),[[189]](#cite_note-191) and the [Indianapolis Hebrew Congregation](/wiki/Indianapolis_Hebrew_Congregation "Indianapolis Hebrew Congregation") (1856).[[190]](#cite_note-192) Newer establishments reflect the city’s growing religious diversity, including [Masjid Al Mu'mineen](/wiki/Masjid_Al_Mu%27mineen "Masjid Al Mu'mineen") (2001), the [Hindu Temple of Central Indiana](/wiki/Hindu_Temple_of_Central_Indiana "Hindu Temple of Central Indiana") (2006),[[191]](#cite_note-193) and the [Indianapolis Indiana Temple](/wiki/Indianapolis_Indiana_Temple "Indianapolis Indiana Temple") (2015), located in [Carmel](/wiki/Carmel,_Indiana "Carmel, Indiana").[[192]](#cite_note-194) The [First Church of Cannabis](/wiki/First_Church_of_Cannabis "First Church of Cannabis") was established in response to Indiana’s [Religious Freedom Restoration Act](/wiki/Religious_Freedom_Restoration_Act_(Indiana) "Religious Freedom Restoration Act (Indiana)") in 2015.[[181]](#cite_note-Lantzer-183)

Religious denominations headquartered in the Indianapolis area include the [Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)](/wiki/Christian_Church_(Disciples_of_Christ) "Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)"),[[185]](#cite_note-Seay-187) the [Free Methodist Church](/wiki/Free_Methodist_Church "Free Methodist Church")[[193]](#cite_note-195) the [Lutheran Ministerium and Synod – USA](/wiki/Lutheran_Ministerium_and_Synod_%E2%80%93_USA "Lutheran Ministerium and Synod – USA"), and the [Wesleyan Church](/wiki/Wesleyan_Church "Wesleyan Church").[[185]](#cite_note-Seay-187) Indianapolis is the seat of two [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese_of_Indianapolis_(disambiguation) "Diocese of Indianapolis (disambiguation)").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Indianapolis](/wiki/Economy_of_Indianapolis "Economy of Indianapolis")
