Indianapolis anchors the [28th-largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._metropolitan_areas_by_GDP "List of U.S. metropolitan areas by GDP") metropolitan economy in the United States, with a [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of US$199.2 billion in 2023.[[20]](#cite_note-FRED-20) Major regional exports include [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals"), motor vehicle parts, medical equipment and supplies, engine and power equipment, and aircraft products and parts.[[12]](#cite_note-Indy_Chamber-12) According to the [U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics](/wiki/United_States_Bureau_of_Labor_Statistics "United States Bureau of Labor Statistics"), the largest employment sectors in the 11-county [Indianapolis metropolitan area](/wiki/Indianapolis_metropolitan_area "Indianapolis metropolitan area") are trade, transportation, and utilities; education and health services; professional and business services; government; leisure and hospitality; and manufacturing. The area's unemployment rate was 3.7 percent in February 2024.[[21]](#cite_note-industries-21)

Three [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies are headquartered in Indianapolis: insurance firm [Elevance Health](/wiki/Elevance_Health "Elevance Health"), pharmaceutical manufacturer [Eli Lilly](/wiki/Eli_Lilly_and_Company "Eli Lilly and Company"), and agricultural science company [Corteva](/wiki/Corteva "Corteva").[[194]](#cite_note-Corteva-196) Other notable companies based in the city include, among others, [Allison Transmission](/wiki/Allison_Transmission "Allison Transmission"), [Barnes & Thornburg](/wiki/Barnes_%26_Thornburg "Barnes & Thornburg"), [Elanco](/wiki/Elanco "Elanco"), [Indiana University Health](/wiki/Indiana_University_Health "Indiana University Health"), [OneAmerica Financial](/wiki/OneAmerica_Financial "OneAmerica Financial"), [Republic Airways Holdings](/wiki/Republic_Airways_Holdings "Republic Airways Holdings"), [Simon Property Group](/wiki/Simon_Property_Group "Simon Property Group"), [Steak 'n Shake](/wiki/Steak_%27n_Shake "Steak 'n Shake"), and [Wheaton World Wide Moving](/wiki/Wheaton_World_Wide_Moving "Wheaton World Wide Moving").

Indianapolis's central location and extensive highway, rail, and air cargo infrastructure have positioned the city as a major logistics hub. In 2020, the region supported approximately 4,300 logistics establishments employing nearly 110,000 workers.[[195]](#cite_note-197) [FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx") operates its National Hub at [Indianapolis International Airport](/wiki/Indianapolis_International_Airport "Indianapolis International Airport"), while [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") maintains a substantial regional presence.[[196]](#cite_note-Logistics-198)[[197]](#cite_note-199)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Eli_Lilly_Corporate_Center%2C_Indianapolis%2C_Indiana%2C_USA.jpg/250px-Eli_Lilly_Corporate_Center%2C_Indianapolis%2C_Indiana%2C_USA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eli_Lilly_Corporate_Center,_Indianapolis,_Indiana,_USA.jpg)

Indianapolis-based [Eli Lilly and Company](/wiki/Eli_Lilly_and_Company "Eli Lilly and Company"), the city's largest private employer

The metropolitan area anchors one of the nation's largest [life sciences](/wiki/List_of_life_sciences "List of life sciences") clusters, particularly in pharmaceuticals and agricultural biosciences.[[198]](#cite_note-BIO2020-200) Depending on methodology, the sector employs between 21,200 and 28,700 workers across nearly 350 companies in the region.[[199]](#cite_note-TEConomy_BioCrossroads-201) Eli Lilly is the city's largest private employer, with approximately 11,000 workers engaged in research, manufacturing, and corporate administration.[[200]](#cite_note-Manufacturers-202) Other major life sciences employers include [Corteva](/wiki/Corteva "Corteva"), [Fortrea](/wiki/Fortrea "Fortrea"), and [Roche](/wiki/Roche "Roche")'s North American diagnostics headquarters.

Manufacturing has historically played a central role in Indianapolis's economy; however, following mid-20th-century [deindustrialization](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization"), the city experienced substantial industrial job losses and economic restructuring. Once rivaling [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") as an early center of [automobile manufacturing](/wiki/Automobile_manufacturing "Automobile manufacturing"), Indianapolis hosted more than 60 automakers in the early 20th century, including luxury marques such as [Duesenberg](/wiki/Duesenberg "Duesenberg"), [Marmon](/wiki/Marmon_Motor_Car_Company "Marmon Motor Car Company"), and [Stutz Motor Company](/wiki/Stutz_Motor_Company "Stutz Motor Company").[[64]](#cite_note-automaker-64)[[201]](#cite_note-automobile_industry-203) The city is commonly identified as part of the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt"), reflecting broader patterns of industrial decline in the Midwestern United States.[[202]](#cite_note-204)
Despite these shifts, Indianapolis remains a significant manufacturing center. [Allison Transmission](/wiki/Allison_Transmission "Allison Transmission") and [Rolls-Royce North America](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_North_America "Rolls-Royce North America") operate major headquarters and production facilities in the city, supporting thousands of jobs in advanced manufacturing, aerospace, and propulsion systems.[[200]](#cite_note-Manufacturers-202) Other major manufacturers include [Allegion](/wiki/Allegion "Allegion") and [RTX Corporation](/wiki/RTX_Corporation "RTX Corporation").[[200]](#cite_note-Manufacturers-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Indianapolis%2C_October_5%2C_2024_-_38.jpg/250px-Indianapolis%2C_October_5%2C_2024_-_38.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis,_October_5,_2024_-_38.jpg)

[Indiana Convention Center](/wiki/Indiana_Convention_Center "Indiana Convention Center") and [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium") in 2024

The city's [hospitality industry](/wiki/Hospitality_industry "Hospitality industry") has grown alongside the expansion of its convention business.[[203]](#cite_note-conventions-205)[[204]](#cite_note-Zeigler-206) Indianapolis receives approximately 29.2 million visitors annually, generating US$5.6 billion in economic impact and supporting nearly 83,000 jobs.[[205]](#cite_note-207) Major hospitality assets are concentrated in [downtown Indianapolis](/wiki/Downtown_Indianapolis "Downtown Indianapolis"), including the [Indiana Convention Center](/wiki/Indiana_Convention_Center "Indiana Convention Center") and [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium"), which host large national conventions such as [FDIC International](/wiki/FDIC_International "FDIC International"), [Gen Con](/wiki/Gen_Con "Gen Con"), and the [National FFA Organization](/wiki/National_FFA_Organization "National FFA Organization") Convention.[[206]](#cite_note-208)

Indianapolis has experienced growth in high-technology employment since the 2010s, reflecting broader economic diversification trends.[[207]](#cite_note-209)[[208]](#cite_note-210) The metropolitan area supports roughly 28,500 information technology–related jobs, with major employers including [Infosys](/wiki/Infosys "Infosys"), [Salesforce Marketing Cloud](/wiki/Salesforce_Marketing_Cloud "Salesforce Marketing Cloud"), and [Genesys](/wiki/Genesys_(company) "Genesys (company)").[[209]](#cite_note-211)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

### Visual arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Visual arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/IMA_-_All_you_need_is_rust%21_%282592098693%29.jpg/250px-IMA_-_All_you_need_is_rust%21_%282592098693%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:IMA_-_All_you_need_is_rust!_(2592098693).jpg)

[Robert Indiana](/wiki/Robert_Indiana "Robert Indiana")'s *[LOVE](/wiki/Love_(Indianapolis) "Love (Indianapolis)")* at the Indianapolis Museum of Art in 2008

The [Indianapolis Museum of Art at Newfields](/wiki/Indianapolis_Museum_of_Art_at_Newfields "Indianapolis Museum of Art at Newfields") is the city's primary art museum, with a permanent collection of approximately 54,000 works. Among the [largest](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums") and oldest art museums in the United States, it traces its origins to the Art Association of Indianapolis, founded in 1883.[[210]](#cite_note-212) The museum occupies a 152-acre (62 ha) campus which also includes [Oldfields–Lilly House & Gardens](/wiki/Oldfields "Oldfields") and the [Virginia B. Fairbanks Art & Nature Park](/wiki/Virginia_B._Fairbanks_Art_%26_Nature_Park "Virginia B. Fairbanks Art & Nature Park"). [Downtown Indianapolis](/wiki/Downtown_Indianapolis "Downtown Indianapolis") is home to the [Eiteljorg Museum](/wiki/Eiteljorg_Museum "Eiteljorg Museum"), which maintains a collection focused on [visual arts by indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Visual_arts_by_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Visual arts by indigenous peoples of the Americas") and [Western American Art](/wiki/Western_American_Art "Western American Art"). The museum also hosts lectures, artist residencies, special exhibitions, and public programs throughout the year.[[211]](#cite_note-213)

Founded in 1902, the [Herron School of Art and Design](/wiki/Herron_School_of_Art_and_Design "Herron School of Art and Design") is one of the city's most prominent art institutions. Its early faculty included [Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionist "Impressionist") painters associated with the [Hoosier Group](/wiki/Hoosier_Group "Hoosier Group"). Since 2005, the school has been housed in [Eskenazi Hall](/wiki/Sidney_and_Lois_Eskenazi_Hall "Sidney and Lois Eskenazi Hall") at IU Indianapolis, which contains five public galleries and hosts exhibitions, lectures, and educational programs throughout the year.[[212]](#cite_note-214)

The [Indy Art Center](/wiki/Indy_Art_Center "Indy Art Center"), located in [Broad Ripple](/wiki/Broad_Ripple_Village,_Indianapolis "Broad Ripple Village, Indianapolis"), houses the Marilyn K. Glick School of Art and includes galleries and a sculpture garden. The center offers year-round programming, including art classes, exhibitions, art fairs, and community outreach initiatives.[[213]](#cite_note-215) Additional gallery and studio spaces operate at the [Garfield Park Arts Center](/wiki/Garfield_Park_Arts_Center "Garfield Park Arts Center"), the [Harrison Center](/wiki/Harrison_Center "Harrison Center") in the [Old Northside](/wiki/Old_Northside_Historic_District "Old Northside Historic District"), and the [Murphy Art Center](/wiki/Murphy_Art_Center "Murphy Art Center") in [Fountain Square](/wiki/Fountain_Square,_Indianapolis "Fountain Square, Indianapolis"). These venues participate in the city's monthly [First Friday](/wiki/First_Friday_(public_event) "First Friday (public event)") arts events.

Public art plays a significant role in the city's cultural landscape. As of 2021, Indianapolis had more than [3,000 documented works of public art](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_Indianapolis "List of public art in Indianapolis"), over one-third of which are located in the downtown area. Major collections include the [Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis Public Art Collection](/wiki/Indiana_University%E2%80%93Purdue_University_Indianapolis_Public_Art_Collection "Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis Public Art Collection"), the [Indiana Statehouse Public Art Collection](/wiki/Indiana_Statehouse_Public_Art_Collection "Indiana Statehouse Public Art Collection"), and installations along the [Indianapolis Cultural Trail](/wiki/Indianapolis_Cultural_Trail "Indianapolis Cultural Trail"). [Murals](/wiki/Mural "Mural") constitute the most common form of public art in the city.[[214]](#cite_note-216)

In recent years, grassroots and artist-led initiatives have gained increased visibility. [Ganggang](/wiki/Ganggang "Ganggang") has emerged since 2020 as a prominent supporter of [Black artists](/wiki/African-American_art "African-American art") in Indianapolis. Its annual art fair, BUTTER, is a multi-day exhibition held over [Labor Day](/wiki/Labor_Day "Labor Day") weekend that features regional and national artists and has become a notable event in the city's contemporary arts scene.[[215]](#cite_note-217)[[216]](#cite_note-218)[[217]](#cite_note-219)[[218]](#cite_note-220)

### Performing arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Performing arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Indianapolis_Symphony_Orchestra.jpg/250px-Indianapolis_Symphony_Orchestra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_Symphony_Orchestra.jpg)

The [Indianapolis Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Indianapolis_Symphony_Orchestra "Indianapolis Symphony Orchestra") performing at [Hilbert Circle Theatre](/wiki/Hilbert_Circle_Theatre "Hilbert Circle Theatre") in 2019

Several of Indianapolis's most prominent performing arts venues and organizations are concentrated in and around the downtown area, including the [Hilbert Circle Theatre](/wiki/Hilbert_Circle_Theatre "Hilbert Circle Theatre")[[219]](#cite_note-221), home to the [Indianapolis Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Indianapolis_Symphony_Orchestra "Indianapolis Symphony Orchestra")[[220]](#cite_note-222), and the [Indiana Theatre](/wiki/Indiana_Theatre_(Indianapolis,_Indiana) "Indiana Theatre (Indianapolis, Indiana)"), which houses the [Indiana Repertory Theatre](/wiki/Indiana_Repertory_Theatre "Indiana Repertory Theatre").[[221]](#cite_note-223) Additional venues near the central business district include [The Cabaret](/wiki/The_Cabaret "The Cabaret"),[[222]](#cite_note-224) the [Indianapolis Artsgarden](/wiki/Indianapolis_Artsgarden "Indianapolis Artsgarden"),[[223]](#cite_note-225) [Phoenix Theatre](/wiki/Phoenix_Theatre_(Indianapolis) "Phoenix Theatre (Indianapolis)"),[[224]](#cite_note-226) the [Slippery Noodle Inn](/wiki/Slippery_Noodle_Inn "Slippery Noodle Inn"),[[225]](#cite_note-227) and Everwise Amphitheater at [White River State Park](/wiki/White_River_State_Park "White River State Park"), the city's largest outdoor venue.[[226]](#cite_note-228)

The [Mass Ave Cultural Arts District](/wiki/Mass_Ave_Cultural_Arts_District "Mass Ave Cultural Arts District") is a central hub for live performance and theater. It includes [Old National Centre](/wiki/Old_National_Centre "Old National Centre"), the city’s oldest stagehouse, which opened in 1910 and contains the Murat Theatre, Egyptian Room, and Corinthian Hall.[[227]](#cite_note-229) Mass Ave also contains the [Athenæum](/wiki/Athen%C3%A6um_(Das_Deutsche_Haus) "Athenæum (Das Deutsche Haus)") and several smaller theaters—Basile, District, and IF—which collectively host the annual [Indianapolis Theatre Fringe Festival](/wiki/Indianapolis_Theatre_Fringe_Festival "Indianapolis Theatre Fringe Festival") (IndyFringe).[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231) Community-based companies such as Footlite Musicals and the [Epilogue Players](/wiki/Epilogue_Players "Epilogue Players") operate at Hedback Community Theatre in the [Herron–Morton](/wiki/Herron%E2%80%93Morton_Place_Historic_District "Herron–Morton Place Historic District") neighborhood.[[230]](#cite_note-232)[[231]](#cite_note-233)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Madame_Walker_Theatre_Center.jpg/250px-Madame_Walker_Theatre_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madame_Walker_Theatre_Center.jpg)

[Madam Walker Legacy Center](/wiki/Madam_Walker_Legacy_Center "Madam Walker Legacy Center") opened on [Indiana Avenue](/wiki/Indiana_Avenue "Indiana Avenue") in 1927 as a cultural center for the city's African American community.[[232]](#cite_note-234)

The [Madam Walker Legacy Center](/wiki/Madam_Walker_Legacy_Center "Madam Walker Legacy Center") opened in 1927 on [Indiana Avenue](/wiki/Indiana_Avenue "Indiana Avenue") as a cultural center for the city's African American community.[[233]](#cite_note-235) Its theater hosted vaudeville performances and anchored the local jazz scene from the 1920s through the 1960s. The corridor, commonly known as "The Avenue", produced or attracted influential musicians including [David Baker](/wiki/David_Baker_(composer) "David Baker (composer)"), [Slide Hampton](/wiki/Slide_Hampton "Slide Hampton"), [Freddie Hubbard](/wiki/Freddie_Hubbard "Freddie Hubbard"), [J. J. Johnson](/wiki/J._J._Johnson "J. J. Johnson"), [James Spaulding](/wiki/James_Spaulding "James Spaulding"), and the Montgomery Brothers—[Buddy](/wiki/Buddy_Montgomery "Buddy Montgomery"), [Monk](/wiki/Monk_Montgomery "Monk Montgomery"), and guitarist [Wes Montgomery](/wiki/Wes_Montgomery "Wes Montgomery").[[234]](#cite_note-Montgomery-236) Montgomery is widely regarded as one of the most influential jazz guitarists of all time[[234]](#cite_note-Montgomery-236)[[235]](#cite_note-237) and is closely associated with the development of the city’s "Naptown sound".[[236]](#cite_note-238)

Indianapolis supports a broad range of professional and community-based performing arts organizations, including the [Indianapolis Baroque Orchestra](/wiki/Indianapolis_Baroque_Orchestra "Indianapolis Baroque Orchestra"),[[237]](#cite_note-239) [Indianapolis Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Indianapolis_Chamber_Orchestra "Indianapolis Chamber Orchestra"),[[238]](#cite_note-240) [Indianapolis Men's Chorus](/wiki/Indianapolis_Men%27s_Chorus "Indianapolis Men's Chorus"),[[239]](#cite_note-241) [Indianapolis Opera](/wiki/Indianapolis_Opera "Indianapolis Opera"),[[240]](#cite_note-242) and the [Indianapolis Youth Orchestra](/wiki/Indianapolis_Youth_Orchestra "Indianapolis Youth Orchestra"). Additional venues include Butler University’s [Clowes Memorial Hall](/wiki/Clowes_Memorial_Hall "Clowes Memorial Hall"),[[241]](#cite_note-243) the [Melody Inn](/wiki/Melody_Inn_(nightclub) "Melody Inn (nightclub)") in [Butler–Tarkington](/wiki/Butler%E2%80%93Tarkington,_Indianapolis "Butler–Tarkington, Indianapolis"), and the Frank and Katrina Basile Opera Center and [Jazz Kitchen](/wiki/Jazz_Kitchen "Jazz Kitchen") in [Meridian–Kessler](/wiki/Meridian-Kessler,_Indianapolis "Meridian-Kessler, Indianapolis").[[242]](#cite_note-244) The neighborhoods of [Broad Ripple](/wiki/Broad_Ripple_Village,_Indianapolis "Broad Ripple Village, Indianapolis") and [Fountain Square](/wiki/Fountain_Square,_Indianapolis "Fountain Square, Indianapolis") are particularly associated with local live music.[[243]](#cite_note-245)[[244]](#cite_note-246)

Indianapolis is home to several national and international music organizations, including the [American Piano Awards](/wiki/American_Piano_Awards "American Piano Awards"),[[245]](#cite_note-247) [Bands of America](/wiki/Bands_of_America "Bands of America"), [Drum Corps International](/wiki/Drum_Corps_International "Drum Corps International"), and the [Percussive Arts Society](/wiki/Percussive_Arts_Society "Percussive Arts Society").[[246]](#cite_note-248)[[247]](#cite_note-249) Major recurring events include the [Drum Corps International World Class Championships](/wiki/Drum_Corps_International_World_Class_Champions "Drum Corps International World Class Champions"), the [Indianapolis Early Music Festival](/wiki/Indianapolis_Early_Music "Indianapolis Early Music"),[[248]](#cite_note-250) and [Indy Jazz Fest](/wiki/Indy_Jazz_Fest "Indy Jazz Fest").[[249]](#cite_note-251) The quadrennial [International Violin Competition of Indianapolis](/wiki/International_Violin_Competition_of_Indianapolis "International Violin Competition of Indianapolis") is regarded as one of the most prestigious classical music competitions in the world.[[250]](#cite_note-252)

### Literary arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Literary arts")]

During the late nineteenth and early twentieth century, Indianapolis emerged as a center of the [Golden Age of Indiana Literature](/wiki/Golden_Age_of_Indiana_Literature "Golden Age of Indiana Literature"). Prominent writers based in the city during this period included poet [James Whitcomb Riley](/wiki/James_Whitcomb_Riley "James Whitcomb Riley") and novelists [Booth Tarkington](/wiki/Booth_Tarkington "Booth Tarkington") and [Meredith Nicholson](/wiki/Meredith_Nicholson "Meredith Nicholson"), who gained national recognition for their work.[[13]](#cite_note-NPS-13)[[251]](#cite_note-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Mass_Ave.jpg/250px-Mass_Ave.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mass_Ave.jpg)

A mural memorializing [Kurt Vonnegut](/wiki/Kurt_Vonnegut "Kurt Vonnegut") in the [Mass Ave Cultural Arts District](/wiki/Mass_Ave_Cultural_Arts_District "Mass Ave Cultural Arts District"), completed by local artist Pamela Bliss in 2011

In the twentieth century, Indianapolis produced internationally acclaimed novelist [Kurt Vonnegut](/wiki/Kurt_Vonnegut "Kurt Vonnegut"), whose darkly satirical work, including *[Slaughterhouse-Five](/wiki/Slaughterhouse-Five "Slaughterhouse-Five")*, often incorporated the city as a setting or featured Indianapolis-born characters.[[252]](#cite_note-254)[[253]](#cite_note-Vonnegut-255) Vonnegut credited Indianapolis with shaping his humor and perspective, stating that all his work was rooted in the city.[[254]](#cite_note-Memorial_Library-256)

Indianapolis has also been central to African American literary contributions. Poet [Mari Evans](/wiki/Mari_Evans "Mari Evans"), a leading figure in the [Black Arts Movement](/wiki/Black_Arts_Movement "Black Arts Movement"), lived and worked in the city, influencing generations of writers and earning widespread recognition for her work.[[255]](#cite_note-257)

Contemporary literary culture includes bestselling [young adult fiction](/wiki/Young_adult_fiction "Young adult fiction") author [John Green](/wiki/John_Green "John Green"), whose 2012 novel *[The Fault in Our Stars](/wiki/The_Fault_in_Our_Stars "The Fault in Our Stars")* is set in Indianapolis and reflects the city’s local character.[[256]](#cite_note-258)

Key literary sites in the city include the [James Whitcomb Riley Museum Home](/wiki/James_Whitcomb_Riley_Museum_Home "James Whitcomb Riley Museum Home"), the [Ray Bradbury Center](/wiki/Ray_Bradbury_Center "Ray Bradbury Center") at [IU Indianapolis](/wiki/IU_Indianapolis "IU Indianapolis"), and the [Kurt Vonnegut Museum and Library](/wiki/Kurt_Vonnegut_Museum_and_Library "Kurt Vonnegut Museum and Library").[[254]](#cite_note-Memorial_Library-256)

### Attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Attractions")]

See also: [List of attractions and events in Indianapolis](/wiki/List_of_attractions_and_events_in_Indianapolis "List of attractions and events in Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Bucky_the_T._Rex_1.jpg/250px-Bucky_the_T._Rex_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bucky_the_T._Rex_1.jpg)

"[Bucky](/wiki/Specimens_of_Tyrannosaurus#"Bucky":_TCM_2001.90.1 "Specimens of Tyrannosaurus")", a juvenile *[Tyrannosaurus](/wiki/Tyrannosaurus "Tyrannosaurus")* specimen at the Children's Museum of Indianapolis in 2010

[The Children's Museum of Indianapolis](/wiki/The_Children%27s_Museum_of_Indianapolis "The Children's Museum of Indianapolis") is the world's largest children's museum with 433,000 square feet (40,227.02 m2) of exhibit space and a collection of over 120,000 artifacts.[[257]](#cite_note-259)[[258]](#cite_note-260) Due to its leadership and innovations, the museum is a world leader in its field.[[259]](#cite_note-261) In 2023, it was one of the region's most popular attractions, drawing about 1.2 million visitors.[[260]](#cite_note-Attractions-262)

The [Indianapolis Zoo](/wiki/Indianapolis_Zoo "Indianapolis Zoo") houses more than 1,400 animals of 235 species while the adjoining [White River Gardens](/wiki/White_River_Gardens "White River Gardens") contains more than 50,000 plants of nearly 3,000 species, respectively.[[261]](#cite_note-2020_Annual_Report-263) The zoo is a leader in animal conservation and research, recognized for its biennial [Indianapolis Prize](/wiki/Indianapolis_Prize "Indianapolis Prize") award.[[262]](#cite_note-264) It is among the largest privately funded zoos in the U.S.[[263]](#cite_note-265) and one of the city's most visited attractions, with 1.1 million guests in 2023.[[260]](#cite_note-Attractions-262)

Located inside the [Indianapolis Motor Speedway](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway "Indianapolis Motor Speedway"), the [Indianapolis Motor Speedway Museum](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway_Museum "Indianapolis Motor Speedway Museum") exhibits an extensive collection of [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing") memorabilia showcasing various motorsports and automotive history.[[264]](#cite_note-266) Daily grounds and track tours originate from the museum. Located at the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") headquarters, the [NCAA Hall of Champions](/wiki/NCAA_Hall_of_Champions "NCAA Hall of Champions") contains exhibits on [collegiate athletics in the U.S.](/wiki/College_athletics_in_the_United_States "College athletics in the United States")[[265]](#cite_note-Opsahl_Verderame-267)

The [Benjamin Harrison Presidential Site](/wiki/Benjamin_Harrison_Presidential_Site "Benjamin Harrison Presidential Site") includes the archives and restored home of the 23rd president of the U.S., [Benjamin Harrison](/wiki/Benjamin_Harrison "Benjamin Harrison").[[266]](#cite_note-268) Harrison is buried about 3 miles (4.8 km) north of the site at [Crown Hill Cemetery](/wiki/Crown_Hill_Cemetery "Crown Hill Cemetery"). Other notable interments at the cemetery include three [U.S. vice presidents](/wiki/List_of_vice_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of vice presidents of the United States") ([Fairbanks](/wiki/Charles_W._Fairbanks "Charles W. Fairbanks"), [Hendricks](/wiki/Thomas_A._Hendricks "Thomas A. Hendricks"), and [Marshall](/wiki/Thomas_R._Marshall "Thomas R. Marshall")), notorious American gangster [John Dillinger](/wiki/John_Dillinger "John Dillinger"),[[267]](#cite_note-269) and [First Lady](/wiki/First_Lady_of_the_United_States "First Lady of the United States") [Caroline Harrison](/wiki/Caroline_Harrison "Caroline Harrison").[[268]](#cite_note-270) State-specific historical institutions based in Indianapolis include the [Indiana Historical Society](/wiki/Indiana_Historical_Society "Indiana Historical Society"), [Indiana Humanities](/wiki/Indiana_Humanities "Indiana Humanities"), the [Indiana Jewish Historical Society](/wiki/Indiana_Jewish_Historical_Society "Indiana Jewish Historical Society"), [Indiana Landmarks](/wiki/Indiana_Landmarks "Indiana Landmarks"),[[269]](#cite_note-271) the [Indiana Medical History Museum](/wiki/Indiana_Medical_History_Museum "Indiana Medical History Museum"), and the [Indiana State Museum](/wiki/Indiana_State_Museum "Indiana State Museum").

The [Indiana World War Memorial Historic District](/wiki/Indiana_World_War_Memorial_Historic_District "Indiana World War Memorial Historic District") in downtown Indianapolis is home to more than a dozen memorials and monuments to United States veterans, including the national headquarters of the [American Legion](/wiki/American_Legion "American Legion"), the [Indiana War Memorial Museum](/wiki/Indiana_War_Memorial_Museum "Indiana War Memorial Museum"), and the [Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument](/wiki/Soldiers%27_and_Sailors%27_Monument_(Indianapolis) "Soldiers' and Sailors' Monument (Indianapolis)").[[270]](#cite_note-272) Combined, it is the largest war memorial project in the United States,[[271]](#cite_note-macleod-273) encompassing 24 acres (9.7 ha).[[272]](#cite_note-Mitchell-274) Other notable sites in the city include [Crown Hill National Cemetery](/wiki/Crown_Hill_National_Cemetery "Crown Hill National Cemetery"), the [Indiana 9/11 Memorial](/wiki/Indiana_9/11_Memorial "Indiana 9/11 Memorial"), the [Medal of Honor Memorial](/wiki/Medal_of_Honor_Memorial_(Indianapolis) "Medal of Honor Memorial (Indianapolis)"), and the [USS *Indianapolis* National Memorial](/wiki/USS_Indianapolis_(CA-35)#Memorials "USS Indianapolis (CA-35)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/COLISEUMedited.jpg/250px-COLISEUMedited.jpg)](/wiki/File:COLISEUMedited.jpg)

[Indiana Farmers Coliseum](/wiki/Indiana_Farmers_Coliseum "Indiana Farmers Coliseum") during the 2015 [Indiana State Fair](/wiki/Indiana_State_Fair "Indiana State Fair")

Indianapolis hosts numerous annual fairs, festivals, and parades.[[273]](#cite_note-festivals-275) The largest is the [Indiana State Fair](/wiki/Indiana_State_Fair "Indiana State Fair"), regularly drawing more than 800,000 attendees each August. Held during the "[Month of May](/wiki/Month_of_May_(Indianapolis_500) "Month of May (Indianapolis 500)")", the [500 Festival](/wiki/500_Festival "500 Festival") features over 30 programs and events leading up to the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500").[[260]](#cite_note-Attractions-262) Other notable events include the [Indy Pride Festival](/wiki/Indy_Pride_Festival "Indy Pride Festival") and the [Marion County Fair](/wiki/Marion_County_Fair_(Indiana) "Marion County Fair (Indiana)") in June,[[274]](#cite_note-276)[[275]](#cite_note-277) the [Indiana Black Expo](/wiki/Indiana_Black_Expo "Indiana Black Expo") Summer Celebration in July,[[276]](#cite_note-278) the [Historic Irvington Halloween Festival](/wiki/Historic_Irvington_Halloween_Festival "Historic Irvington Halloween Festival") in October,[[277]](#cite_note-279) and the [Circle of Lights](/wiki/Circle_of_Lights "Circle of Lights") during the [holiday season](/wiki/Holiday_season "Holiday season").[[278]](#cite_note-280)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Indianapolis_City_Market_interior_-_2021.jpg/250px-Indianapolis_City_Market_interior_-_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_City_Market_interior_-_2021.jpg)

[Indianapolis City Market](/wiki/Indianapolis_City_Market "Indianapolis City Market"), founded in 1821

Indianapolis has an emerging food scene as well as established eateries.[[279]](#cite_note-281) Founded in 1821 as the city's [public market](/wiki/Marketplaces#Types "Marketplaces"), the [Indianapolis City Market](/wiki/Indianapolis_City_Market "Indianapolis City Market") has served the community from its current building since 1886. Before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the City Market was home to meat and vegetable vendors. As consumer habits evolved and residents moved from the central city, City Market transitioned from a traditional marketplace to a [food hall](/wiki/Food_hall "Food hall").[[280]](#cite_note-282) The AMP and The Garage food halls opened in 2021.[[281]](#cite_note-283)

Situated in the [Corn Belt](/wiki/Corn_Belt "Corn Belt"), Indianapolis has maintained close ties to farming and food production. [Urban agriculture](/wiki/Urban_agriculture "Urban agriculture") in the city dates to the 1930s, when [Flanner House](/wiki/Flanner_House "Flanner House") began teaching Black arrivals how to farm on vacant lots during the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)"). Within a few years, more than 200 families were tending 600 garden plots on nearly 100 acres (40 ha) of land on the city's near north side.[[282]](#cite_note-284) Urban agriculture has made a comeback in recent years in an effort to alleviate [food deserts](/wiki/Food_deserts "Food deserts").[[283]](#cite_note-285) According to the city's Office of Sustainability, there were 129 community farms and gardens in 2020.[[284]](#cite_note-Thrive-286) As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit), several [farmers' markets](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market") have been established throughout Indianapolis.[[285]](#cite_note-287)

Distinctive local dishes include [pork tenderloin sandwiches](/wiki/Pork_tenderloin_sandwich "Pork tenderloin sandwich"),[[286]](#cite_note-288) [sugar cream pie](/wiki/Sugar_cream_pie "Sugar cream pie"), and [beef Manhattan](/wiki/Beef_Manhattan "Beef Manhattan"), invented in Indianapolis.[[287]](#cite_note-289) [Wonder Bread](/wiki/Wonder_Bread "Wonder Bread") and [Choc-Ola](/wiki/Choc-Ola "Choc-Ola") also originate in the city. Longstanding local eateries include [Long's Bakery](/wiki/Long%27s_Bakery "Long's Bakery"), [Mug-n-Bun](/wiki/Mug-n-Bun "Mug-n-Bun"), Shapiro's Deli,[[288]](#cite_note-290) [The Workingman's Friend](/wiki/The_Workingman%27s_Friend "The Workingman's Friend"), and [St. Elmo Steak House](/wiki/St._Elmo_Steak_House "St. Elmo Steak House").[[289]](#cite_note-291) St. Elmo was recognized with the [America's Classics](/wiki/List_of_James_Beard_America%27s_Classics "List of James Beard America's Classics") award by the [James Beard Foundation](/wiki/James_Beard_Foundation "James Beard Foundation") in 2012.[[290]](#cite_note-292) In 2008, the Indianapolis metropolitan area had the highest concentration of [chain restaurants](/wiki/Chain_restaurants "Chain restaurants") per capita in the U.S.[[291]](#cite_note-293) Locally based chains include [MCL Restaurant & Bakery](/wiki/MCL_Restaurant_%26_Bakery "MCL Restaurant & Bakery"), [Noble Roman's](/wiki/Noble_Roman%27s "Noble Roman's"), and [Steak 'n Shake](/wiki/Steak_%27n_Shake "Steak 'n Shake"). The city's growing immigrant population has contributed to a rise in [global cuisine](/wiki/Global_cuisine "Global cuisine"), with some 800 ethnic restaurants opening in recent years.[[292]](#cite_note-294)

In 2016, *[Condé Nast Traveler](/wiki/Cond%C3%A9_Nast_Traveler "Condé Nast Traveler")* named Indianapolis the "most underrated food city in the U.S.", while ranking Milktooth as one of the best restaurants in the world.[[293]](#cite_note-295)[[294]](#cite_note-296) *[Food & Wine](/wiki/Food_%26_Wine "Food & Wine")* called Indianapolis the "rising star of the Midwest".[[295]](#cite_note-297)[[296]](#cite_note-298) Several Indianapolis chefs and restaurateurs have been semifinalists in the [James Beard Foundation Awards](/wiki/James_Beard_Foundation_Award "James Beard Foundation Award") in recent years.[[297]](#cite_note-299)[[298]](#cite_note-300) [Microbreweries](/wiki/Microbreweries "Microbreweries") have become a staple in the city, increasing fivefold since 2009.[[299]](#cite_note-301) There are now about 50 craft brewers in Indianapolis, with [Sun King Brewing](/wiki/Sun_King_Brewing "Sun King Brewing") being the largest.[[300]](#cite_note-302)

### Film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Film and television")]

Main category: [Films set in Indianapolis](/wiki/Category:Films_set_in_Indianapolis "Category:Films set in Indianapolis")

Main category: [Television shows set in Indianapolis](/wiki/Category:Television_shows_set_in_Indianapolis "Category:Television shows set in Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/The_Hilbert_Circle_Theatre%2C_pictured_at_dusk%2C_in_Indianapolis%2C_Indiana%27s_capital_city.jpg/250px-The_Hilbert_Circle_Theatre%2C_pictured_at_dusk%2C_in_Indianapolis%2C_Indiana%27s_capital_city.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Hilbert_Circle_Theatre,_pictured_at_dusk,_in_Indianapolis,_Indiana%27s_capital_city.jpg)

[Hilbert Circle Theatre](/wiki/Hilbert_Circle_Theatre "Hilbert Circle Theatre"), the first purpose-built [movie palace](/wiki/Movie_palace "Movie palace") in Indianapolis[[301]](#cite_note-303)

Indianapolis natives have left a mark on the entertainment industry, most notably during the [Classical Hollywood cinema](/wiki/Classical_Hollywood_cinema "Classical Hollywood cinema") era. [James Baskett](/wiki/James_Baskett "James Baskett") received an [Academy Honorary Award](/wiki/Academy_Honorary_Award "Academy Honorary Award") in [1948](/wiki/20th_Academy_Awards "20th Academy Awards") for his role in [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney")'s *[Song of the South](/wiki/Song_of_the_South "Song of the South")*, becoming the first Black male Oscar recipient. [Sid Grauman](/wiki/Sid_Grauman "Sid Grauman"), one of the founders of the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences"), received an Academy Honorary Award in [1949](/wiki/20th_Academy_Awards "20th Academy Awards"), recognized for raising the standard for film exhibition. Perhaps the most famous actor from the Indianapolis area is Academy Award-nominee, [Steve McQueen](/wiki/Steve_McQueen "Steve McQueen"), who was born in [Beech Grove](/wiki/Beech_Grove,_Indiana "Beech Grove, Indiana"). Other Academy Award nominees from the city include costume designer [Gloria Gresham](/wiki/Gloria_Gresham "Gloria Gresham"), actress [Marjorie Main](/wiki/Marjorie_Main "Marjorie Main"), and actor [Clifton Webb](/wiki/Clifton_Webb "Clifton Webb").[[302]](#cite_note-304)

The city's sports venues have served as backdrops for such films as *[Hoosiers](/wiki/Hoosiers_(film) "Hoosiers (film)")* (1986) and *[Eight Men Out](/wiki/Eight_Men_Out "Eight Men Out")* (1988).[[303]](#cite_note-305) The city's largest contribution to [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"), the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500"), has influenced entertainment for decades, [referenced in film, television, video games, and other media](/wiki/Indianapolis_500_in_film_and_media "Indianapolis 500 in film and media").[[304]](#cite_note-306) Three motion pictures filmed at the [Indianapolis Motor Speedway](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway "Indianapolis Motor Speedway") include *[Speedway](/wiki/Speedway_(1929_film) "Speedway (1929 film)")* (1929), *[To Please a Lady](/wiki/To_Please_a_Lady "To Please a Lady")* (1950), and *[Winning](/wiki/Winning_(film) "Winning (film)")* (1969).[[305]](#cite_note-307) Other motion pictures at least partially filmed in the city include *[Going All the Way](/wiki/Going_All_the_Way "Going All the Way")* (1997), *[Palindromes](/wiki/Palindromes_(film) "Palindromes (film)")* (2004), *[Saving Star Wars](/wiki/Saving_Star_Wars "Saving Star Wars")* (2004), *[Amanda](/wiki/Amanda_(2009_film) "Amanda (2009 film)")* (2009), *[Walter](/wiki/Walter_(2015_film) "Walter (2015 film)")* (2015), *[The MisEducation of Bindu](/wiki/The_MisEducation_of_Bindu "The MisEducation of Bindu")* (2019),[[306]](#cite_note-308) *[Athlete A](/wiki/Athlete_A "Athlete A")* (2020), and *[Our Father](/wiki/Our_Father_(2022_film) "Our Father (2022 film)")* (2022). *Hoosiers* and *[Ringling Brothers Parade Film](/wiki/Ringling_Brothers_Parade_Film "Ringling Brothers Parade Film")* (1902) were added to the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") in 2001 and 2021, respectively.[[307]](#cite_note-309)[[308]](#cite_note-310)

Television programs that have been shot on location in the city include *[100 Days to Indy](/wiki/100_Days_to_Indy "100 Days to Indy")*, *[American Ninja Warrior](/wiki/American_Ninja_Warrior "American Ninja Warrior")*,[[309]](#cite_note-311) *[Antiques Roadshow](/wiki/Antiques_Roadshow_(American_TV_program) "Antiques Roadshow (American TV program)")*,[[310]](#cite_note-312) *[College GameDay](/wiki/College_GameDay_(football_TV_program) "College GameDay (football TV program)")*,[[311]](#cite_note-313) *[Cops](/wiki/Cops_(TV_series) "Cops (TV series)")*,[[312]](#cite_note-314) *[Diners, Drive-Ins and Dives](/wiki/Diners,_Drive-Ins_and_Dives "Diners, Drive-Ins and Dives")*,[[313]](#cite_note-Film-315) *[Extreme Makeover: Home Edition](/wiki/Extreme_Makeover:_Home_Edition "Extreme Makeover: Home Edition"),*[[314]](#cite_note-316) *[Gaycation](/wiki/Gaycation_(TV_series) "Gaycation (TV series)")*,[[315]](#cite_note-317) *[Ghost Hunters](/wiki/Ghost_Hunters_(TV_series) "Ghost Hunters (TV series)")*,[[316]](#cite_note-318) *[Good Bones](/wiki/Good_Bones_(TV_series) "Good Bones (TV series)")*,[[317]](#cite_note-319) *[Good Morning America](/wiki/Good_Morning_America "Good Morning America")*,[[318]](#cite_note-320) *[Hard Knocks](/wiki/Hard_Knocks_(documentary_series) "Hard Knocks (documentary series)")*,[[319]](#cite_note-321) *[House Hunters](/wiki/House_Hunters "House Hunters")*,[[320]](#cite_note-322) *[Late Night with Jimmy Fallon](/wiki/Late_Night_with_Jimmy_Fallon "Late Night with Jimmy Fallon")*,[[321]](#cite_note-323) *[Man v. Food](/wiki/Man_v._Food "Man v. Food")*,[[313]](#cite_note-Film-315) *[Parks and Recreation](/wiki/Parks_and_Recreation "Parks and Recreation")*,[[322]](#cite_note-324)[[323]](#cite_note-325) *[Say I Do](/wiki/Say_I_Do_(TV_series) "Say I Do (TV series)")*,[[324]](#cite_note-326) *[SportsCenter](/wiki/SportsCenter "SportsCenter")*,[[325]](#cite_note-327) *[Today](/wiki/Today_(American_TV_program) "Today (American TV program)")*,[[326]](#cite_note-328) and *[What Would You Do?](/wiki/What_Would_You_Do%3F_(2008_TV_program) "What Would You Do? (2008 TV program)")*[[327]](#cite_note-329)

Annual [film festivals](/wiki/Film_festival "Film festival") held in Indianapolis include the Circle City Film Festival, [Heartland International Film Festival](/wiki/Heartland_International_Film_Festival "Heartland International Film Festival"),[[328]](#cite_note-330) [Indy Film Fest](/wiki/Indy_Film_Fest "Indy Film Fest"),[[273]](#cite_note-festivals-275) the [Indianapolis Jewish Film Festival](/wiki/Indianapolis_Jewish_Film_Festival "Indianapolis Jewish Film Festival"), and the Indianapolis LGBT Film Festival. Founded in 2018, the Indy Shorts International Film Festival is one of 34 film festivals in the world used to qualify for the Academy Awards.[[329]](#cite_note-331)

Film Indy was established in 2016 to support local visual artists, filmmakers, and aspiring filmmakers; recruit film and television-related marketing opportunities to the region, and provide resources for producers interested in filming in the city.[[313]](#cite_note-Film-315) Since 2016, more than 350 film and media projects have been produced in the Indianapolis region with a collective economic impact of $24.1 million and the creation of 1,900 local jobs.[[330]](#cite_note-332)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sports in Indianapolis](/wiki/Sports_in_Indianapolis "Sports in Indianapolis")

### Professional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Professional")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Super_Bowl-6_%286833620123%29.jpg/250px-Super_Bowl-6_%286833620123%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Super_Bowl-6_(6833620123).jpg)

[Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium"), home of the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts"), during [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Bankers_Life_Fieldhouse%2C_Indian%C3%A1polis%2C_Estados_Unidos%2C_2012-10-22%2C_DD_02.jpg/250px-Bankers_Life_Fieldhouse%2C_Indian%C3%A1polis%2C_Estados_Unidos%2C_2012-10-22%2C_DD_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bankers_Life_Fieldhouse,_Indian%C3%A1polis,_Estados_Unidos,_2012-10-22,_DD_02.jpg)

[Gainbridge Fieldhouse](/wiki/Gainbridge_Fieldhouse "Gainbridge Fieldhouse"), home to the [Indiana Pacers](/wiki/Indiana_Pacers "Indiana Pacers") and [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever")

The [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") have been based in the city since [relocating from Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Colts_relocation_to_Indianapolis "Baltimore Colts relocation to Indianapolis") in 1984. The Colts' tenure in Indianapolis has produced 11 division championships, two conference titles, and two [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") appearances. [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") inductees [Tony Dungy](/wiki/Tony_Dungy "Tony Dungy") and [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") led the team to win [Super Bowl XLI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLI "Super Bowl XLI") in 2007.[[331]](#cite_note-333) [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium") replaced the team's first home, the [RCA Dome](/wiki/RCA_Dome "RCA Dome"), in 2008.[[332]](#cite_note-334)

Founded in 1967, the [Indiana Pacers](/wiki/Indiana_Pacers "Indiana Pacers") began in the [American Basketball Association](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association "American Basketball Association") (ABA), joining the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) when the leagues [merged](/wiki/ABA-NBA_merger "ABA-NBA merger") in 1976. Before joining the NBA, the Pacers won three ABA division titles and three ABA championships ([1970](/wiki/1970_ABA_Playoffs "1970 ABA Playoffs"), [1972](/wiki/1972_ABA_Playoffs "1972 ABA Playoffs"), [1973](/wiki/1973_ABA_Playoffs "1973 ABA Playoffs")), led by [Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame") inductees [Mel Daniels](/wiki/Mel_Daniels "Mel Daniels"), [Roger Brown](/wiki/Roger_Brown_(basketball,_born_1942) "Roger Brown (basketball, born 1942)"), and [George McGinnis](/wiki/George_McGinnis "George McGinnis"). Since the merger, the Pacers have made 10 appearances in the [NBA Eastern Conference finals](/wiki/NBA_Eastern_Conference_finals "NBA Eastern Conference finals"). In [2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Indiana_Pacers_season "1999–2000 Indiana Pacers season"), Hall of Fame inductees [Larry Bird](/wiki/Larry_Bird "Larry Bird") and [Reggie Miller](/wiki/Reggie_Miller "Reggie Miller") led the team to its first [NBA Finals](/wiki/2000_NBA_Finals "2000 NBA Finals") appearance.[[333]](#cite_note-335) The team returned to the NBA Finals in [2025](/wiki/2025_NBA_Finals "2025 NBA Finals"), led by [Rick Carlisle](/wiki/Rick_Carlisle "Rick Carlisle") and [Tyrese Haliburton](/wiki/Tyrese_Haliburton "Tyrese Haliburton").

Since their debut in 2000, the [Indiana Fever](/wiki/Indiana_Fever "Indiana Fever") of the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") (WNBA) have won three conference titles, made three appearances in the [WNBA Finals](/wiki/WNBA_Finals "WNBA Finals"), and won one championship. Hall of fame inductees [Lin Dunn](/wiki/Lin_Dunn "Lin Dunn") and [Tamika Catchings](/wiki/Tamika_Catchings "Tamika Catchings") led the Fever to their first championship title in [2012](/wiki/2012_WNBA_Finals "2012 WNBA Finals").[[334]](#cite_note-336) The Fever and Pacers share [Gainbridge Fieldhouse](/wiki/Gainbridge_Fieldhouse "Gainbridge Fieldhouse"), which replaced [Market Square Arena](/wiki/Market_Square_Arena "Market Square Arena") in 1999.[[335]](#cite_note-337)

The [Indianapolis Indians](/wiki/Indianapolis_Indians "Indianapolis Indians") are a [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") team of the [International League](/wiki/International_League "International League") and the [Triple-A](/wiki/Triple-A_(baseball) "Triple-A (baseball)") affiliate of the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates").[[336]](#cite_note-338) Established in 1902, the Indians are among the oldest teams in Minor League Baseball.[[337]](#cite_note-339) The Indians have won 26 division titles, 14 league titles, and seven championships, most recently in 2000. [Victory Field](/wiki/Victory_Field "Victory Field") replaced [Bush Stadium](/wiki/Bush_Stadium "Bush Stadium") as the team's home in 1996.[[338]](#cite_note-340)

[Indy Eleven](/wiki/Indy_Eleven "Indy Eleven"), a professional soccer team in the second-division [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship"), play their home matches at [Carroll Stadium](/wiki/IU_Michael_A._Carroll_Track_%26_Soccer_Stadium "IU Michael A. Carroll Track & Soccer Stadium").[[339]](#cite_note-341) The [Fishers Event Center](/wiki/Fishers_Event_Center "Fishers Event Center") in the suburb of [Fishers](/wiki/Fishers,_Indiana "Fishers, Indiana") is home to the [Indy Fuel](/wiki/Indy_Fuel "Indy Fuel") (a minor league ice hockey team in the [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL")),[[340]](#cite_note-342) [Indy Ignite](/wiki/Indy_Ignite "Indy Ignite") (a member of the [Pro Volleyball Federation](/wiki/Pro_Volleyball_Federation "Pro Volleyball Federation")), and [Fishers Freight](/wiki/Fishers_Freight "Fishers Freight") (a member of the [Indoor Football League](/wiki/Indoor_Football_League "Indoor Football League")).

### Amateur

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Amateur")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Austin_Etherington_reverse_layup.jpg/250px-Austin_Etherington_reverse_layup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austin_Etherington_reverse_layup.jpg)

[Butler Bulldogs men's basketball](/wiki/Butler_Bulldogs_men%27s_basketball "Butler Bulldogs men's basketball") at [Hinkle Fieldhouse](/wiki/Hinkle_Fieldhouse "Hinkle Fieldhouse")

Indianapolis has been called the "Amateur Sports Capital of the World".[[45]](#cite_note-Britannica-45)[[341]](#cite_note-343) The [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA), the main governing body for U.S. collegiate sports, and the [National Federation of State High School Associations](/wiki/National_Federation_of_State_High_School_Associations "National Federation of State High School Associations") are based in the city.[[265]](#cite_note-Opsahl_Verderame-267) Indianapolis is home to two [NCAA athletic conferences](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_conferences "List of NCAA conferences"): the [Horizon League](/wiki/Horizon_League "Horizon League") ([D-I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I")) and the [Great Lakes Valley Conference](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Valley_Conference "Great Lakes Valley Conference") ([D-II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II")). The city is also home to three national sport governing bodies, as recognized by the [U.S. Olympic & Paralympic Committee](/wiki/United_States_Olympic_%26_Paralympic_Committee "United States Olympic & Paralympic Committee"): [USA Football](/wiki/USA_Football "USA Football"); [USA Gymnastics](/wiki/USA_Gymnastics "USA Gymnastics"); and [USA Track & Field](/wiki/USA_Track_%26_Field "USA Track & Field").[[342]](#cite_note-344)

Two NCAA D-I athletic programs are based in Indianapolis: the [Butler Bulldogs](/wiki/Butler_Bulldogs "Butler Bulldogs") of the [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference") and the [IU Indy Jaguars](/wiki/IU_Indy_Jaguars "IU Indy Jaguars") of the Horizon League.[[343]](#cite_note-IUPUI-345) The [University of Indianapolis](/wiki/University_of_Indianapolis "University of Indianapolis") is a D-II school; the [Greyhounds](/wiki/Indianapolis_Greyhounds "Indianapolis Greyhounds") compete in the Great Lakes Valley Conference. [Marian University](/wiki/Marian_University_(Indiana) "Marian University (Indiana)") athletics compete in the National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics's [Crossroads League](/wiki/Crossroads_League "Crossroads League").

Traditionally, Butler's [Hinkle Fieldhouse](/wiki/Hinkle_Fieldhouse "Hinkle Fieldhouse") was the hub for [Hoosier hysteria](/wiki/Hoosier_hysteria "Hoosier hysteria"), a general excitement for the game of basketball throughout the state, specifically the [Indiana High School Boys Basketball Tournament](/wiki/Indiana_High_School_Boys_Basketball_Tournament "Indiana High School Boys Basketball Tournament").[[344]](#cite_note-346) Hinkle, a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), opened in 1928 as the world's largest basketball arena, with seating for 15,000.[[345]](#cite_note-347) It is regarded as "Indiana's Basketball Cathedral".[[346]](#cite_note-348) The [Indiana High School Athletic Association](/wiki/Indiana_High_School_Athletic_Association "Indiana High School Athletic Association") is based in Indianapolis.[[347]](#cite_note-349)

### Motorsports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Motorsports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Defense.gov_photo_essay_120527-A-MG757-086.jpg/250px-Defense.gov_photo_essay_120527-A-MG757-086.jpg)](/wiki/File:Defense.gov_photo_essay_120527-A-MG757-086.jpg)

An [open-wheel car](/wiki/Open-wheel_car "Open-wheel car") crossing the Yard of Bricks during practice for the [2012 Indianapolis 500](/wiki/2012_Indianapolis_500 "2012 Indianapolis 500")

Indianapolis is a global center for [auto racing](/wiki/Auto_racing "Auto racing"), home to numerous motorsports facilities and events, two sanctioning bodies ([INDYCAR](/wiki/INDYCAR "INDYCAR") and [United States Auto Club](/wiki/United_States_Auto_Club "United States Auto Club")), and more than 500 motorsports-related companies.[[348]](#cite_note-350) Indianapolis, or Indy, is a [metonym](/wiki/Metonymy "Metonymy") for auto racing, particularly when referring to [American open-wheel car racing](/wiki/American_open-wheel_car_racing "American open-wheel car racing").[[349]](#cite_note-351)

Completed in 1909 as an automotive test track, the [Indianapolis Motor Speedway](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway "Indianapolis Motor Speedway") is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") and the world's largest [sports venue by capacity](/wiki/List_of_sports_venues_by_capacity "List of sports venues by capacity"), with 235,000 permanent seats.[[350]](#cite_note-352) Since 1911, the 2.5-mile-long (4.0 km) rectangular oval has hosted the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500"), an open-wheel automobile race. It is contested as part of the [IndyCar Series](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series") and traditionally held over [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day "Memorial Day") weekend. Steeped in [tradition](/wiki/Indianapolis_500_traditions "Indianapolis 500 traditions"), the race is considered the world's largest single-day sporting event and part of the [Triple Crown of Motorsport](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Motorsport "Triple Crown of Motorsport").[[26]](#cite_note-Keefer-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Davidson-27) The track also hosts the [Grand Prix of Indianapolis](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Indianapolis "Grand Prix of Indianapolis") in May and NASCAR's [Brickyard 400](/wiki/Brickyard_400 "Brickyard 400") and [Pennzoil 250](/wiki/Pennzoil_250 "Pennzoil 250") in July.[[351]](#cite_note-353)

[Lucas Oil Indianapolis Raceway Park](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Indianapolis_Raceway_Park "Lucas Oil Indianapolis Raceway Park") (in nearby [Brownsburg](/wiki/Brownsburg,_Indiana "Brownsburg, Indiana")) contains a 2.5-mile-long (4.0 km) road course, a 4,400-foot-long (1,300 m) [dragstrip](/wiki/Dragstrip "Dragstrip"), and a 0.69-mile-long (1.11 km) [oval short track](/wiki/Oval_track_racing#Short_track "Oval track racing"). Each [Labor Day](/wiki/Labor_Day "Labor Day") weekend, the facility hosts the [NHRA U.S. Nationals](/wiki/NHRA_U.S._Nationals "NHRA U.S. Nationals"), the largest and most prestigious [drag racing](/wiki/Drag_racing "Drag racing") event in the world.[[352]](#cite_note-354)[[353]](#cite_note-355) The city's [Indianapolis Speedrome](/wiki/Indianapolis_Speedrome "Indianapolis Speedrome") is believed to be the oldest operating [figure 8 racing](/wiki/Figure_8_racing "Figure 8 racing") venue in the U.S.[[354]](#cite_note-356)

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Events")]

Beyond its many auto races, Indianapolis hosts numerous other sporting events throughout the year, including the [NFL Scouting Combine](/wiki/NFL_Scouting_Combine "NFL Scouting Combine") in February, the [500 Festival Mini-Marathon](/wiki/500_Festival_Mini-Marathon "500 Festival Mini-Marathon") in May,[[355]](#cite_note-357) the [Circle City Classic](/wiki/Circle_City_Classic "Circle City Classic") in September,[[356]](#cite_note-358) the [Monumental Marathon](/wiki/Indianapolis_Monumental_Marathon "Indianapolis Monumental Marathon") in November, and the [Big Ten Football Championship Game](/wiki/Big_Ten_Football_Championship_Game "Big Ten Football Championship Game") and [Indy Classic](/wiki/Indy_Classic "Indy Classic") in December. Indianapolis also regularly hosts the [NCAA Division I men's](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") and [women's basketball](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I women's basketball tournament") Final Fours, most recently in [2021](/wiki/2021_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2021 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") and [2016](/wiki/2016_NCAA_Division_I_women%27s_basketball_tournament "2016 NCAA Division I women's basketball tournament"), respectively.[[357]](#cite_note-359)

Notable past events hosted in the city include the [U.S. Clay Court Championships](/wiki/U.S._Clay_Court_Championships "U.S. Clay Court Championships") (1969–1987), the [National Sports Festival](/wiki/U.S._Olympic_Festival "U.S. Olympic Festival") (1982), the [NBA All-Star Game](/wiki/NBA_All-Star_Game "NBA All-Star Game") ([1985](/wiki/1985_NBA_All-Star_Game "1985 NBA All-Star Game") and [2024](/wiki/2024_NBA_All-Star_Game "2024 NBA All-Star Game")), the [Pan American Games](/wiki/Pan_American_Games "Pan American Games") ([1987](/wiki/1987_Pan_American_Games "1987 Pan American Games")), the [Indianapolis Tennis Championships](/wiki/Indianapolis_Tennis_Championships "Indianapolis Tennis Championships") (1988–2009), the [World Artistic Gymnastics Championships](/wiki/World_Artistic_Gymnastics_Championships "World Artistic Gymnastics Championships") ([1991](/wiki/1991_World_Artistic_Gymnastics_Championships "1991 World Artistic Gymnastics Championships")), [WrestleMania VIII](/wiki/WrestleMania_VIII "WrestleMania VIII") (1992), the [World Rowing Championships](/wiki/World_Rowing_Championships "World Rowing Championships") ([1994](/wiki/1994_World_Rowing_Championships "1994 World Rowing Championships")), the [United States Grand Prix](/wiki/United_States_Grand_Prix "United States Grand Prix") (2000–2007), the [World Police and Fire Games](/wiki/World_Police_and_Fire_Games "World Police and Fire Games") (2001), the [FIBA Basketball World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Basketball World Cup") ([2002](/wiki/2002_FIBA_World_Championship "2002 FIBA World Championship")), [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI") (2012), the [College Football Playoff National Championship](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff_National_Championship "College Football Playoff National Championship") ([2022](/wiki/2022_College_Football_Playoff_National_Championship "2022 College Football Playoff National Championship")), and the [WNBA All-Star Game](/wiki/WNBA_All-Star_Game "WNBA All-Star Game") ([2025](/wiki/2025_WNBA_All-Star_Game "2025 WNBA All-Star Game")).

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Government of Indianapolis](/wiki/Government_of_Indianapolis "Government of Indianapolis")

See also: [List of mayors of Indianapolis](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Indianapolis "List of mayors of Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/City_County_Building_of_Indianapolis.jpg/250px-City_County_Building_of_Indianapolis.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_County_Building_of_Indianapolis.jpg)

[City-County Building](/wiki/City-County_Building_(Indianapolis) "City-County Building (Indianapolis)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/IndianaStateHouse1.jpg/250px-IndianaStateHouse1.jpg)](/wiki/File:IndianaStateHouse1.jpg)

[Indiana Statehouse](/wiki/Indiana_Statehouse "Indiana Statehouse")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Night_view%2C_Birch_Bayh_Federal_Building%2C_Indianapolis%2C_Indiana_LCCN2010719407.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Night_view%2C_Birch_Bayh_Federal_Building%2C_Indianapolis%2C_Indiana_LCCN2010719407.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Night_view,_Birch_Bayh_Federal_Building,_Indianapolis,_Indiana_LCCN2010719407.tif)

[Birch Bayh Federal Building and United States Courthouse](/wiki/Birch_Bayh_Federal_Building_and_United_States_Courthouse "Birch Bayh Federal Building and United States Courthouse")

Indianapolis—officially the Consolidated City of Indianapolis and [Marion County](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana")—has a [consolidated city-county](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county") form of government, a status it has held since 1970 under [Indiana Code](/wiki/Indiana_Code "Indiana Code")'s [Unigov](/wiki/Unigov "Unigov") provision. Many functions of the municipal and county governments are consolidated, though some remain separate.[[8]](#cite_note-Unigov-8) The city has a [strong mayor–council](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government "Mayor–council government") system of government which oversees six administrative departments. Marion County also contains some 60 taxing units, nine separate [civil township](/wiki/Civil_township "Civil township") governments, and seven special-purpose [municipal corporations](/wiki/Municipally_owned_corporation "Municipally owned corporation").[[358]](#cite_note-360)[[359]](#cite_note-361)

The executive branch is headed by an elected mayor who serves as the chief executive of both the city and county.[[360]](#cite_note-Mayor-362) [Indianapolis City-County Council](/wiki/Indianapolis_City-County_Council "Indianapolis City-County Council") is the legislative body and consists of 25 members, all of whom represent geographic districts. The mayor and council members are elected to unlimited four-year terms.[[360]](#cite_note-Mayor-362)[[361]](#cite_note-363) Executive and legislative functions are based from the [City-County Building](/wiki/City-County_Building_(Indianapolis) "City-County Building (Indianapolis)"). The judiciary consists of a circuit court and superior court with four divisions and 32 judges.[[8]](#cite_note-Unigov-8) Each of the county's nine civil townships elects its own [township trustee](/wiki/Indiana_township_trustee "Indiana township trustee"), three-member board, assessor, and a constable and small claims court judge, all of whom serve four-year terms.[[92]](#cite_note-Townships-92)

Since its move from [Corydon](/wiki/Corydon,_Indiana "Corydon, Indiana") in 1825, Indianapolis has served as the [capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") and seat of Indiana's state government. The [Indiana Statehouse](/wiki/Indiana_Statehouse "Indiana Statehouse") houses the executive, legislative, and judicial branches of state government, including the office of the [Governor of Indiana](/wiki/Governor_of_Indiana "Governor of Indiana"), the [Indiana General Assembly](/wiki/Indiana_General_Assembly "Indiana General Assembly"), and the [Indiana Supreme Court](/wiki/Indiana_Supreme_Court "Indiana Supreme Court"). Most state departments and agencies are headquartered in the neighboring [Indiana Government Center](/wiki/Indiana_Government_Center_North "Indiana Government Center North") complex.[[362]](#cite_note-364) The [Indiana Governor's Residence](/wiki/Indiana_Governor%27s_Residence "Indiana Governor's Residence") is on [Meridian Street](/wiki/Meridian_Street_(Indianapolis) "Meridian Street (Indianapolis)") in the [Butler–Tarkington](/wiki/Butler-Tarkington,_Indianapolis "Butler-Tarkington, Indianapolis") neighborhood, about 5 miles (8.0 km) north of downtown. In the [Indiana House of Representatives](/wiki/Indiana_House_of_Representatives "Indiana House of Representatives"), Indianapolis is split between 16 districts.[[363]](#cite_note-365) In the [Indiana Senate](/wiki/Indiana_Senate "Indiana Senate"), the city is split between nine districts.[[364]](#cite_note-366)

The [Birch Bayh Federal Building and U.S. Courthouse](/wiki/Birch_Bayh_Federal_Building_and_United_States_Courthouse "Birch Bayh Federal Building and United States Courthouse") houses the [U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Indiana](/wiki/U.S._District_Court_for_the_Southern_District_of_Indiana "U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Indiana"). Most federal field offices are located in the [Minton-Capehart Federal Building](/wiki/Minton-Capehart_Federal_Building "Minton-Capehart Federal Building"). From 1906 to 1991, the [U.S. Army](/wiki/U.S._Army "U.S. Army") operated [Fort Benjamin Harrison](/wiki/Fort_Benjamin_Harrison "Fort Benjamin Harrison") in neighboring [Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence,_Indiana "Lawrence, Indiana"). About 5,000 federal employees work for the [Defense Finance and Accounting Service](/wiki/Defense_Finance_and_Accounting_Service "Defense Finance and Accounting Service"), headquartered near the former base.[[365]](#cite_note-367) Indianapolis is split between two of Indiana's nine [congressional districts](/wiki/Congressional_district "Congressional district"): [Indiana's 7th congressional district](/wiki/Indiana%27s_7th_congressional_district "Indiana's 7th congressional district"), represented by [André Carson](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Carson "André Carson"), and [Indiana's 6th congressional district](/wiki/Indiana%27s_6th_congressional_district "Indiana's 6th congressional district"), represented by [Jefferson Shreve](/wiki/Jefferson_Shreve "Jefferson Shreve").

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Politics")]

Further information: [Marion County, Indiana § Politics](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana#Politics "Marion County, Indiana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Indianapolis_Mayor_2023.svg/250px-Indianapolis_Mayor_2023.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_Mayor_2023.svg)

[2023 Indianapolis mayoral election](/wiki/2023_Indianapolis_mayoral_election "2023 Indianapolis mayoral election") by precinct:  
**[Hogsett](/wiki/Joe_Hogsett "Joe Hogsett") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")):**      50–60%      60–70%      70–80%      80–90%      >90%  
**[Shreve](/wiki/Jefferson_Shreve "Jefferson Shreve") ([R](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")):**      50–60%      60–70%      70–80%  
**Tie:**      50%  
      No votes

Until fairly recently, Indianapolis was considered one of the most [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") major cities in the U.S.[[366]](#cite_note-368)[[71]](#cite_note-politics-71) According to 2014 research published in the *[American Political Science Review](/wiki/American_Political_Science_Review "American Political Science Review")*, the city's policy preferences are less conservative than the national mean when compared with other large U.S. cities.[[367]](#cite_note-369) While Indianapolis as a whole leans Democratic, the southern third of the city, consisting of [Decatur](/wiki/Decatur_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Decatur Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Perry](/wiki/Perry_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Perry Township, Marion County, Indiana"), and [Franklin](/wiki/Franklin_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Franklin Township, Marion County, Indiana") townships, trends Republican.[[368]](#cite_note-370)

[Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") held the mayor's office for 32 years (1967–1999), and controlled the City-County Council from its inception in 1970 to 2003.[[71]](#cite_note-politics-71) In the [2000 United States presidential election](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election "2000 United States presidential election"), Marion County voters narrowly selected [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") over [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") by a margin of 1.3%, but voted in favor of [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") by a margin of 1.9% in the [2004 United States presidential election](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election "2004 United States presidential election"). Presidential election results have increasingly favored Democrats, with Marion County voters selecting [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") over [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in the [2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"), 63.3–34.3%.[[369]](#cite_note-371) Incumbent mayor [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Joe Hogsett](/wiki/Joe_Hogsett "Joe Hogsett") faced Republican State Senator [Jim Merritt](/wiki/Jim_Merritt_(American_politician) "Jim Merritt (American politician)") and [Libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(United_States) "Libertarian Party (United States)") Doug McNaughton in the [2019 Indianapolis mayoral election](/wiki/2019_Indianapolis_mayoral_election "2019 Indianapolis mayoral election"). Hogsett was elected to a second term, with 72% of the vote.[[370]](#cite_note-372) The [2019 City-County Council elections](/wiki/2019_Indianapolis_City-County_Council_election "2019 Indianapolis City-County Council election") expanded Democratic control of the council, flipping six seats to hold a 20–5 [supermajority](/wiki/Supermajority "Supermajority") over Republicans.[[371]](#cite_note-373)

### Public safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Public safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Indianapolis_Fire_Department_Station_13.jpg/250px-Indianapolis_Fire_Department_Station_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_Fire_Department_Station_13.jpg)

[Indianapolis Fire Department](/wiki/Indianapolis_Fire_Department "Indianapolis Fire Department") Station 13

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Indiana_Metropolitan_Police_Department_Dodge_Charger_-_June_2022_-_Sarah_Stierch.jpg/250px-Indiana_Metropolitan_Police_Department_Dodge_Charger_-_June_2022_-_Sarah_Stierch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indiana_Metropolitan_Police_Department_Dodge_Charger_-_June_2022_-_Sarah_Stierch.jpg)

[Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department](/wiki/Indianapolis_Metropolitan_Police_Department "Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department") vehicle

The [Indianapolis Fire Department](/wiki/Indianapolis_Fire_Department "Indianapolis Fire Department") (IFD) comprises seven battalions with 44 fire stations.[[372]](#cite_note-374) IFD provides [mutual aid](/wiki/Mutual_aid_(emergency_services) "Mutual aid (emergency services)") to the excluded municipalities of [Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence,_Indiana "Lawrence, Indiana") and [Speedway](/wiki/Speedway,_Indiana "Speedway, Indiana"), as well as [Decatur](/wiki/Decatur_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Decatur Township, Marion County, Indiana"), [Pike](/wiki/Pike_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Pike Township, Marion County, Indiana"), and [Wayne](/wiki/Wayne_Township,_Marion_County,_Indiana "Wayne Township, Marion County, Indiana") townships (all of which operate separate fire departments). IFD directs operations for Indiana Task Force 1 (IN-TF1), one of 28 [FEMA Urban Search and Rescue Task Force](/wiki/FEMA_Urban_Search_and_Rescue_Task_Force "FEMA Urban Search and Rescue Task Force") teams in the U.S.[[373]](#cite_note-IFD-375)

The [Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department](/wiki/Indianapolis_Metropolitan_Police_Department "Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department") (IMPD) is the city's primary [law enforcement agency](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency"). IMPD's jurisdiction covers Marion County, excluding the municipalities of [Beech Grove](/wiki/Beech_Grove,_Indiana "Beech Grove, Indiana"), Lawrence, [Southport](/wiki/Southport,_Indiana "Southport, Indiana"), and Speedway (all of which operate separate forces). In 2020, IMPD had 1,700 sworn police personnel and 250 civilian employees across six districts.[[374]](#cite_note-376) In 2022, the Community Justice Campus opened, housing the Marion County Sheriff's Office, a new courthouse, jail, and mental health and substance abuse clinic.[[375]](#cite_note-377)

The [Indiana National Guard](/wiki/Indiana_National_Guard "Indiana National Guard")'s major command units and joint headquarters staff are based at the former [Stout Army Air Field](/wiki/Stout_Army_Air_Field "Stout Army Air Field") on the city's southwest side.[[376]](#cite_note-378)

Unlike other major Midwest cities like [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), the homicide rate for Indianapolis remained below the national average throughout the 1990s.[[377]](#cite_note-379) Homicides hit a spike in 1998 when the city reached 162 murders. Murders drastically decreased in the following years but spiked again in 2006 with 153 murders.[[378]](#cite_note-380) Until 2019, annual criminal homicide numbers had grown each year since 2011, reaching record highs from 2015 to 2018.[[379]](#cite_note-criminal_homicides-381) With 144 criminal homicides, 2015 surpassed 1998 as the year with the most murder investigations in the city. With 159 criminal homicides, 2018 stands as the most violent year on record in the city.[[379]](#cite_note-criminal_homicides-381) [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") data showed a 7% increase in violent crimes committed in Indianapolis, outpacing the rest of the state and country.[[380]](#cite_note-382) Law enforcement has blamed increased violence on a combination of root causes, including poverty, substance abuse, and mental illness.[[381]](#cite_note-383)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in Indianapolis](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Indianapolis "List of schools in Indianapolis")

### Primary and secondary schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Primary and secondary schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Shortridge_High_School_Indianapolis_Aug_2016.jpg/250px-Shortridge_High_School_Indianapolis_Aug_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shortridge_High_School_Indianapolis_Aug_2016.jpg)

Established in 1864, [Shortridge High School](/wiki/Shortridge_High_School "Shortridge High School") is Indiana's oldest free public high school.[[382]](#cite_note-384)

Nine [K–12](/wiki/K%E2%80%9312 "K–12") public [school districts](/wiki/School_district "School district") serve residents of the consolidated city as defined by the U.S. Census Bureau (which includes the balance and included cities, and does not include the excluded cities):[[383]](#cite_note-385)

* [Franklin Township Community School Corporation](/wiki/Franklin_Township_Community_School_Corporation "Franklin Township Community School Corporation")
* [Indianapolis Public Schools](/wiki/Indianapolis_Public_Schools "Indianapolis Public Schools")
* [Metropolitan School District of Decatur Township](/wiki/Metropolitan_School_District_of_Decatur_Township "Metropolitan School District of Decatur Township")
* [Metropolitan School District of Lawrence Township](/wiki/Metropolitan_School_District_of_Lawrence_Township "Metropolitan School District of Lawrence Township")
* [Metropolitan School District of Pike Township](/wiki/Metropolitan_School_District_of_Pike_Township "Metropolitan School District of Pike Township")
* [Metropolitan School District of Warren Township](/wiki/Metropolitan_School_District_of_Warren_Township "Metropolitan School District of Warren Township")
* [Metropolitan School District of Washington Township](/wiki/Metropolitan_School_District_of_Washington_Township "Metropolitan School District of Washington Township")
* [Metropolitan School District of Wayne Township](/wiki/Metropolitan_School_District_of_Wayne_Township "Metropolitan School District of Wayne Township")
* [Perry Township Schools](/wiki/Perry_Township_Schools "Perry Township Schools")

[Beech Grove City Schools](/wiki/Beech_Grove_City_Schools "Beech Grove City Schools") and [Speedway School Town](/wiki/Speedway_School_Town "Speedway School Town") only include sections of excluded cities.

Indianapolis Public Schools is the largest district in the city, enrolling about 23,000 students across 60 schools.[[384]](#cite_note-386) In 2015, the district began contracting with [charter organizations](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") and nonprofit managers to operate failing schools.[[385]](#cite_note-387) About 63% of the district's students attend traditional neighborhood or [magnet schools](/wiki/Magnet_school "Magnet school"), while the remaining 37% are enrolled in independently managed schools.[[386]](#cite_note-Weddle-388)[[387]](#cite_note-389) About 18,000 students are enrolled in tuition-free charter schools sponsored by the Indianapolis Mayor's Office of Education Innovation and Indianapolis Charter School Board.[[388]](#cite_note-390)

Indianapolis is home to two state-supported [residential schools](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school"), the [Indiana School for the Blind and Visually Impaired](/wiki/Indiana_School_for_the_Blind_and_Visually_Impaired "Indiana School for the Blind and Visually Impaired") and [Indiana School for the Deaf](/wiki/Indiana_School_for_the_Deaf "Indiana School for the Deaf"). According to the [Indiana Department of Education](/wiki/Indiana_Department_of_Education "Indiana Department of Education"), about 75 private, parochial, and independent charter schools operate throughout Marion County. Roman Catholic and Christian parochial primary and secondary schools are most prevalent.[[389]](#cite_note-391)[[390]](#cite_note-392) Notable independent private schools include the [International School of Indiana](/wiki/International_School_of_Indiana "International School of Indiana"),[[391]](#cite_note-393) the [Orchard School](/wiki/The_Orchard_School_(Indianapolis) "The Orchard School (Indianapolis)"),[[392]](#cite_note-394) and [Park Tudor School](/wiki/Park_Tudor_School "Park Tudor School")[[393]](#cite_note-395) in [Meridian Hills](/wiki/Meridian_Hills,_Indiana "Meridian Hills, Indiana").[[394]](#cite_note-396)

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Campus_Center_-_IUPUI_-_DSC00526.JPG/250px-Campus_Center_-_IUPUI_-_DSC00526.JPG)](/wiki/File:Campus_Center_-_IUPUI_-_DSC00526.JPG)

The [Campus Center](/wiki/IUPUI_Campus_Center "IUPUI Campus Center") at [Indiana University Indianapolis](/wiki/Indiana_University_Indianapolis "Indiana University Indianapolis")

The city is home to two four-year [public university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") campuses: [Indiana University Indianapolis](/wiki/Indiana_University_Indianapolis "Indiana University Indianapolis") and [Purdue University in Indianapolis](/wiki/Purdue_University_in_Indianapolis "Purdue University in Indianapolis").
