The two institutions jointly operated [Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis](/wiki/Indiana_University%E2%80%93Purdue_University_Indianapolis "Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis") (IUPUI) until its dissolution in 2024.[[343]](#cite_note-IUPUI-345) IU Indianapolis is an [R1](/wiki/List_of_research_universities_in_the_United_States "List of research universities in the United States")[[395]](#cite_note-397) [urban](/wiki/Urban_university "Urban university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") that enrolls about 25,000 students in 330 different bachelor's, master's, and doctoral degree programs. It is home to 17 degree-granting schools, including the main campus of the [IU School of Medicine](/wiki/IU_School_of_Medicine "IU School of Medicine"), the largest medical school by enrollment in the U.S.[[396]](#cite_note-AAMC-398)[[397]](#cite_note-Kirchhoff-399) Purdue University in Indianapolis is considered an extension of [its flagship campus](/wiki/Purdue_University "Purdue University") in West Lafayette.

[Ivy Tech](/wiki/Ivy_Tech_Community_College_of_Indiana "Ivy Tech Community College of Indiana"), Indiana's statewide [community college](/wiki/Community_college "Community college") system, enrolls some 21,000 full-time students at its Downtown Indianapolis campus.[[398]](#cite_note-400) Other public institutions with [satellite campuses](/wiki/Satellite_campus "Satellite campus") in the city include Ball State University's [Estopinal College of Architecture and Planning](/wiki/Estopinal_College_of_Architecture_and_Planning "Estopinal College of Architecture and Planning"),[[399]](#cite_note-401) [Purdue Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/Purdue_Polytechnic_Institute "Purdue Polytechnic Institute"), and [Vincennes University](/wiki/Vincennes_University "Vincennes University").[[400]](#cite_note-402)

Two [secular](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") [private universities](/wiki/Private_universities "Private universities") are based in Indianapolis. Founded in 1855, [Butler University](/wiki/Butler_University "Butler University") serves an enrollment of about 5,000 from its [Butler–Tarkington](/wiki/Butler%E2%80%93Tarkington,_Indianapolis "Butler–Tarkington, Indianapolis") campus.[[401]](#cite_note-403)[[402]](#cite_note-Largest_Colleges_and_Universities-404) [Martin University](/wiki/Martin_University "Martin University"), Indiana's only [Predominantly Black Institution](/wiki/Minority-serving_institution "Minority-serving institution"), was founded in 1977 and is located in the [Martindale–Brightwood](/wiki/Martindale%E2%80%93Brightwood,_Indianapolis "Martindale–Brightwood, Indianapolis") neighborhood.[[403]](#cite_note-405) [Indiana Tech](/wiki/Indiana_Institute_of_Technology "Indiana Institute of Technology") maintains a branch campus in the city.[[404]](#cite_note-406) Two [seminaries](/wiki/Seminaries "Seminaries") are located in the city: [Bishop Simon Bruté College Seminary](/wiki/Bishop_Simon_Brut%C3%A9_College_Seminary "Bishop Simon Bruté College Seminary") and [Christian Theological Seminary](/wiki/Christian_Theological_Seminary "Christian Theological Seminary").[[405]](#cite_note-407) Three religiously affiliated universities based in the city are [Indiana Bible College](/wiki/Indiana_Bible_College "Indiana Bible College"), the [University of Indianapolis](/wiki/University_of_Indianapolis "University of Indianapolis"),[[402]](#cite_note-Largest_Colleges_and_Universities-404) and [Marian University](/wiki/Marian_University_(Indiana) "Marian University (Indiana)").[[402]](#cite_note-Largest_Colleges_and_Universities-404) The [College of Biblical Studies](/wiki/College_of_Biblical_Studies "College of Biblical Studies") and [Indiana Wesleyan University](/wiki/Indiana_Wesleyan_University "Indiana Wesleyan University") operate satellite sites in Indianapolis.[[406]](#cite_note-408)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Libraries")]

Main article: [Indianapolis Public Library](/wiki/Indianapolis_Public_Library "Indianapolis Public Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Indianapolis%2C_October_2%2C_2024_-_021.jpg/250px-Indianapolis%2C_October_2%2C_2024_-_021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis,_October_2,_2024_-_021.jpg)

The Simon Reading Room at [Central Library](/wiki/Central_Library_(Indianapolis) "Central Library (Indianapolis)")

Founded in 1873, the [Indianapolis Public Library](/wiki/Indianapolis_Public_Library "Indianapolis Public Library") (IndyPL) consists of the [Central Library](/wiki/Central_Library_(Indianapolis) "Central Library (Indianapolis)") and 24 branches throughout Marion County.[[407]](#cite_note-409) Central Library's special collections include the Center for Black Literature & Culture, the [Chris Gonzalez Collection](/wiki/Chris_Gonzalez_Collection "Chris Gonzalez Collection"), and the [Nina Mason Pulliam Indianapolis Special Collections Room](/wiki/Nina_Mason_Pulliam_Indianapolis_Special_Collections_Room "Nina Mason Pulliam Indianapolis Special Collections Room"). The library collection contains nearly 1.7 million materials staffed by 410 full-time employees and has a circulation of 14.6 million, making it the ninth largest library by circulation in the U.S.[[408]](#cite_note-410)[[409]](#cite_note-411)

Indianapolis is also home to the [Indiana State Library and Historical Bureau](/wiki/Indiana_State_Library_and_Historical_Bureau "Indiana State Library and Historical Bureau"), the state's largest public library.[[410]](#cite_note-412) Notable academic libraries in the city include IU Indianapolis's [University Library](/wiki/IUPUI_University_Library "IUPUI University Library") and Butler University's [Irwin Library](/wiki/Irwin_Library "Irwin Library").

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in Indianapolis](/wiki/Media_in_Indianapolis "Media in Indianapolis")

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Print")]

Indianapolis's primary daily newspaper is the *[Indianapolis Star](/wiki/The_Indianapolis_Star "The Indianapolis Star")*.[[411]](#cite_note-413) Defunct major newspapers include the *[Indianapolis News](/wiki/Indianapolis_News "Indianapolis News")*, an evening publication which printed its last edition in 1999;[[412]](#cite_note-414) and the *[Indianapolis Times](/wiki/Indianapolis_Times "Indianapolis Times")*, which ceased publication in 1965.[[413]](#cite_note-415) Additional publications include *[Indianapolis Monthly](/wiki/Indianapolis_Monthly "Indianapolis Monthly")*, a regional lifestyle publication;[[414]](#cite_note-416) *[Indianapolis Business Journal](/wiki/Indianapolis_Business_Journal "Indianapolis Business Journal")*, a weekly business newspaper;[[415]](#cite_note-417) and *[NUVO](/wiki/NUVO_(newspaper) "NUVO (newspaper)")*, an [alternative weekly](/wiki/Alternative_newspaper "Alternative newspaper") that became digital-only in 2019.[[416]](#cite_note-418)

Indianapolis's [ethnic media](/wiki/Ethnic_media "Ethnic media") include the *[Indianapolis Recorder](/wiki/Indianapolis_Recorder "Indianapolis Recorder")*, a weekly newspaper that primarily serves the city's African American community;[[417]](#cite_note-419) *[Indiana Minority Business Magazine](/wiki/Indiana_Minority_Business_Magazine "Indiana Minority Business Magazine")*, a quarterly publication;[[418]](#cite_note-420) and *La Voz de Indiana*, a biweekly newspaper printed in English and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language").[[419]](#cite_note-421)

### Broadcast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Broadcast")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/WYXB-WIBC-Offices.jpg/250px-WYXB-WIBC-Offices.jpg)](/wiki/File:WYXB-WIBC-Offices.jpg)

The studios of [WYXB](/wiki/WYXB "WYXB") and [WIBC](/wiki/WIBC_(FM) "WIBC (FM)") at [Emmis Communications](/wiki/Emmis_Communications "Emmis Communications") in 2006

The Indianapolis [television market area](/wiki/Television_market_area "Television market area") is served by 11 full-power stations, including [WTTV](/wiki/WTTV "WTTV") 4 ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")),[[420]](#cite_note-422) [WRTV](/wiki/WRTV "WRTV") 6 ([ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")),[[421]](#cite_note-423) [WISH-TV](/wiki/WISH-TV "WISH-TV") 8 ([The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW")),[[422]](#cite_note-424) [WTHR](/wiki/WTHR "WTHR") 13 ([NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")),[[423]](#cite_note-425) [WFYI-TV](/wiki/WFYI_(TV) "WFYI (TV)") 20 ([PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS")),[[424]](#cite_note-426) [WNDY-TV](/wiki/WNDY-TV "WNDY-TV") 23 ([MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV")), [WHMB-TV](/wiki/WHMB-TV "WHMB-TV") 40 ([Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision")), [WCLJ-TV](/wiki/WCLJ-TV "WCLJ-TV") 42 ([Bounce TV](/wiki/Bounce_TV "Bounce TV")), [WXIN](/wiki/WXIN "WXIN") 59 ([Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")),[[425]](#cite_note-427) [WIPX-TV](/wiki/WIPX-TV "WIPX-TV") 63 ([Ion](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television")), and [WDTI](/wiki/WDTI "WDTI") 69 ([Daystar](/wiki/Daystar_(TV_network) "Daystar (TV network)")). Indianapolis natives [Jane Pauley](/wiki/Jane_Pauley "Jane Pauley") and [David Letterman](/wiki/David_Letterman "David Letterman") launched their [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award")-winning broadcasting careers in [local television](/wiki/Local_programming "Local programming"), Pauley with WISH-TV and Letterman with WTHR, respectively.[[426]](#cite_note-428)[[427]](#cite_note-429) Sports talk program *[The Pat McAfee Show](/wiki/The_Pat_McAfee_Show "The Pat McAfee Show")* broadcasts from the city, airing weekdays on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN").[[428]](#cite_note-430)

Dozens of [commercial](/wiki/Commercial_broadcasting "Commercial broadcasting") [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting") and [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") [radio stations](/wiki/Radio_broadcasting "Radio broadcasting") serve the Indianapolis area, including [WCBK](/wiki/WCBK-FM "WCBK-FM") ([country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music")), [WEDJ](/wiki/WEDJ "WEDJ") ([Regional Mexican](/wiki/Regional_Mexican "Regional Mexican")), [WFBQ](/wiki/WFBQ "WFBQ") ([classic rock](/wiki/Classic_rock "Classic rock")),[[429]](#cite_note-431) [WFMS](/wiki/WFMS "WFMS") (country),[[430]](#cite_note-432) [WHHH](/wiki/WHHH "WHHH") ([urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary_music "Urban contemporary music")), [WIBC](/wiki/WIBC_(FM) "WIBC (FM)") ([news](/wiki/All-news_radio "All-news radio")/[talk](/wiki/Talk_radio "Talk radio")),[[431]](#cite_note-433) [WJJK](/wiki/WJJK "WJJK") ([classic hits](/wiki/Classic_hits "Classic hits")), [WLHK](/wiki/WLHK "WLHK") (country), [WNTS](/wiki/WNTS "WNTS") (classic Regional Mexican), [WNDX](/wiki/WNDX "WNDX") ([mainstream rock](/wiki/Mainstream_rock "Mainstream rock")), [WNTR](/wiki/WNTR "WNTR") ([adult contemporary](/wiki/Adult_contemporary_music "Adult contemporary music")), [WOLT](/wiki/WOLT "WOLT") ([classic alternative](/wiki/Classic_alternative "Classic alternative")), [WSYW](/wiki/WSYW "WSYW") ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") adult contemporary), [WTLC](/wiki/WTLC-FM "WTLC-FM") ([urban adult contemporary](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary")),[[432]](#cite_note-434) [WYXB](/wiki/WYXB "WYXB") (adult contemporary), [WZPL](/wiki/WZPL "WZPL") ([contemporary hits](/wiki/Contemporary_hit_radio "Contemporary hit radio")), and [WZRL](/wiki/WZRL "WZRL") (mainstream urban). Since 1983, WFBQ has been the [flagship](/wiki/Flagship_(broadcasting) "Flagship (broadcasting)") station for the popular nationally [syndicated](/wiki/Radio_syndication "Radio syndication") radio program *[The Bob & Tom Show](/wiki/The_Bob_%26_Tom_Show "The Bob & Tom Show")*.[[433]](#cite_note-435)

[Sports radio](/wiki/Sports_radio "Sports radio") stations include [WFNI](/wiki/WFNI "WFNI") ([ESPN Radio](/wiki/ESPN_Radio "ESPN Radio")),[[434]](#cite_note-436) [WNDE](/wiki/WNDE "WNDE") ([Fox Sports Radio](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Radio "Fox Sports Radio")),[[435]](#cite_note-437) and [WXNT](/wiki/WXNT "WXNT") ([Infinity Sports Network](/wiki/Infinity_Sports_Network "Infinity Sports Network")).[[436]](#cite_note-438) WFNI (formerly WIBC, currently broadcasting on WIBC-HD3 and its FM translators) is the flagship of the [Indianapolis Motor Speedway Radio Network](/wiki/Indianapolis_Motor_Speedway_Radio_Network "Indianapolis Motor Speedway Radio Network").[[437]](#cite_note-439) [Religious](/wiki/Religious_broadcasting "Religious broadcasting") stations include [WBRI](/wiki/WBRI "WBRI"),[[438]](#cite_note-440) [WGNR](/wiki/WGNR-FM "WGNR-FM"), [WWDL](/wiki/WWDL "WWDL"), and [WYHX](/wiki/WYHX "WYHX"). [WICR](/wiki/WICR "WICR") is the [campus radio](/wiki/Campus_radio "Campus radio") station at the [University of Indianapolis](/wiki/University_of_Indianapolis "University of Indianapolis").[[439]](#cite_note-441) [Classical Music Indy](/wiki/Classical_Music_Indy "Classical Music Indy") produces and syndicates [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") programming for WICR.[[440]](#cite_note-442) [Metropolitan Indianapolis Public Media](/wiki/Metropolitan_Indianapolis_Public_Media "Metropolitan Indianapolis Public Media") operates [WFYI-FM](/wiki/WFYI-FM "WFYI-FM"), the region's [NPR](/wiki/National_Public_Radio "National Public Radio") affiliate.[[441]](#cite_note-443)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in Indianapolis](/wiki/Transportation_in_Indianapolis "Transportation in Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Interstates_65_and_70_Indianapolis.jpg/250px-Interstates_65_and_70_Indianapolis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interstates_65_and_70_Indianapolis.jpg)

[Interstates 65](/wiki/Interstate_65_in_Indiana "Interstate 65 in Indiana") and [70](/wiki/Interstate_70_in_Indiana "Interstate 70 in Indiana") run concurrently on the eastern perimeter of [downtown Indianapolis](/wiki/Downtown_Indianapolis "Downtown Indianapolis").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/IndyGo_2496_on_Red_Line_at_Julia_M._Carson_Transit_Center.jpg/250px-IndyGo_2496_on_Red_Line_at_Julia_M._Carson_Transit_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:IndyGo_2496_on_Red_Line_at_Julia_M._Carson_Transit_Center.jpg)

An [IndyGo](/wiki/IndyGo "IndyGo") bus at the [Julia M. Carson Transit Center](/wiki/Julia_M._Carson_Transit_Center "Julia M. Carson Transit Center")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Indianapolis_International_Airport_Civic_Plaza.jpg/250px-Indianapolis_International_Airport_Civic_Plaza.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indianapolis_International_Airport_Civic_Plaza.jpg)

[Indianapolis International Airport](/wiki/Indianapolis_International_Airport "Indianapolis International Airport") Colonel H. Weir Cook Terminal Civic Plaza

Indianapolis's transportation infrastructure consists of a complex network that includes a local public bus system, several private intercity bus providers, [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") passenger rail service, four freight rail lines, [four primary and two auxiliary Interstate Highways](/wiki/List_of_Interstate_Highways_in_Indiana "List of Interstate Highways in Indiana"), two airports, a heliport, bikeshare system, 115 miles (185 km) of bike lanes,[[284]](#cite_note-Thrive-286) and 110 miles (177 km) of trails and greenways.[[442]](#cite_note-transport-444)[[284]](#cite_note-Thrive-286) Private [ridesharing companies](/wiki/Ridesharing_companies "Ridesharing companies") [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft "Lyft") and [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber") as well as [taxicabs](/wiki/Taxicabs "Taxicabs") operate in the city.[[443]](#cite_note-445) Launched in 2018, electric [scooter-sharing systems](/wiki/Scooter-sharing_system "Scooter-sharing system") operating in Indianapolis include [Bird](/wiki/Bird_Global "Bird Global"), [Lime](/wiki/Lime_(transportation_company) "Lime (transportation company)"), and Veo.[[444]](#cite_note-446)

[Urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl") and the absence of a comprehensive regional public transit system have contributed to Indianapolis residents driving more vehicle miles per capita than any other U.S. city.[[445]](#cite_note-Gibson-447) According to the 2016 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), 83.7% of working residents in the city commuted by driving alone, 8.4% carpooled, 1.5% used public transportation, and 1.8% walked. About 1.5% used all other forms of transportation, including taxicab, motorcycle, and bicycle. About 3.1% of working city residents worked at home.[[446]](#cite_note-448) In 2015, 10.5 percent of Indianapolis households lacked a car, which decreased to 8.7 percent in 2016, the same as the national average in that year. Indianapolis averaged 1.63 cars per household in 2016, compared to a national average of 1.8.[[447]](#cite_note-449)

Four primary Interstate Highways intersect the city: [Interstate 65](/wiki/Interstate_65_in_Indiana "Interstate 65 in Indiana"), [Interstate 69](/wiki/Interstate_69_in_Indiana "Interstate 69 in Indiana"), [Interstate 70](/wiki/Interstate_70_in_Indiana "Interstate 70 in Indiana"), and [Interstate 74](/wiki/Interstate_74_in_Indiana "Interstate 74 in Indiana"). The metropolitan area also has two auxiliary Interstate Highways: a [beltway](/wiki/Beltway "Beltway") ([Interstate 465](/wiki/Interstate_465 "Interstate 465")) and [connector](/wiki/Connector_(road) "Connector (road)") ([Interstate 865](/wiki/Interstate_865 "Interstate 865")). The [Indiana Department of Transportation](/wiki/Indiana_Department_of_Transportation "Indiana Department of Transportation") manages all Interstates, [U.S. Highways](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highway_System "United States Numbered Highway System"), and [state roads](/wiki/List_of_state_roads_in_Indiana "List of state roads in Indiana") within the city. The city's Department of Public Works maintains more than 3,400 miles (5,500 km) of local streets in addition to alleys, sidewalks, curbs, and 510 bridges.[[448]](#cite_note-Dwyer-450)

Sidewalks are absent from nearly 2,000 miles (3,200 km) of the city's roadways,[[448]](#cite_note-Dwyer-450) contributing to Indianapolis's low [walkability](/wiki/Walkability "Walkability") among peer U.S. cities.[[449]](#cite_note-451) However, city officials have increased investments in bicycle and pedestrian infrastructure in recent years.[[445]](#cite_note-Gibson-447) About 110 miles (180 km) of trails and greenways form the core of the city's [active transportation](/wiki/Active_mobility "Active mobility") network, connecting into 115 miles (185 km) of on-street bike lanes.[[450]](#cite_note-452)[[284]](#cite_note-Thrive-286) Popular routes include the [Fall Creek Greenway](/wiki/Fall_Creek_Greenway "Fall Creek Greenway"), [Monon Trail](/wiki/Monon_Trail "Monon Trail"), and [Pleasant Run Greenway](/wiki/Pleasant_Run_Greenway "Pleasant Run Greenway").[[442]](#cite_note-transport-444) The privately managed [Indianapolis Cultural Trail](/wiki/Indianapolis_Cultural_Trail "Indianapolis Cultural Trail") operates [Indiana Pacers Bikeshare](/wiki/Indiana_Pacers_Bikeshare "Indiana Pacers Bikeshare"), the city's [bicycle-sharing system](/wiki/Bicycle-sharing_system "Bicycle-sharing system"), which consists of 525 bicycles and 50 stations.[[451]](#cite_note-453)

[IndyGo](/wiki/IndyGo "IndyGo") operates and manages the city's [public bus system](/wiki/Public_transport_bus_service "Public transport bus service"), including [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit"), [microtransit](/wiki/Microtransit "Microtransit"), and [paratransit](/wiki/Paratransit "Paratransit") services. The [Julia M. Carson Transit Center](/wiki/Julia_M._Carson_Transit_Center "Julia M. Carson Transit Center") serves as the downtown hub for 27 of its 31 [fixed routes](/wiki/Transit_bus "Transit bus").[[452]](#cite_note-454)[[442]](#cite_note-transport-444) In 2020, IndyGo's fleet of 212 buses provided about 4.8 million passenger trips (compared with pre-[COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_public_transport "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on public transport") ridership of 9.2 million in 2019).[[453]](#cite_note-455) The [Central Indiana Regional Transportation Authority](/wiki/Central_Indiana_Regional_Transportation_Authority "Central Indiana Regional Transportation Authority") is a quasi-governmental agency that organizes regional [car](/wiki/Carpool "Carpool") and [vanpools](/wiki/Vanpools "Vanpools") and operates three public [shuttle buses](/wiki/Shuttle_bus "Shuttle bus") connecting Indianapolis to employment centers in suburban [Plainfield](/wiki/Plainfield,_Indiana "Plainfield, Indiana") and [Whitestown](/wiki/Whitestown,_Indiana "Whitestown, Indiana").[[454]](#cite_note-456)

[Indianapolis International Airport](/wiki/Indianapolis_International_Airport "Indianapolis International Airport")'s Colonel H. Weir Cook Terminal contains two concourses and 40 gates.[[455]](#cite_note-457) In 2024, the airport served 10.5 million passengers and offered nonstop service to 53 domestic and international destinations.[[456]](#cite_note-458) As home to the second largest [FedEx Express](/wiki/FedEx_Express "FedEx Express") hub in the world, it ranks among the [ten busiest U.S. airports](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_the_United_States#10_busiest_airports_in_the_U.S._by_total_cargo_throughput_(2017) "List of the busiest airports in the United States") in terms of air cargo throughput.[[457]](#cite_note-459) The [Indianapolis Airport Authority](/wiki/Indianapolis_Airport_Authority "Indianapolis Airport Authority") oversees operations at five additional aviation facilities in the region, two of which are located in the city: [Eagle Creek Airpark](/wiki/Eagle_Creek_Airpark "Eagle Creek Airpark") and the [Indianapolis Downtown Heliport](/wiki/Indianapolis_Downtown_Heliport "Indianapolis Downtown Heliport").[[458]](#cite_note-460)

[Indianapolis Union Station](/wiki/Indianapolis_Union_Station "Indianapolis Union Station") is the state's primary [intercity bus](/wiki/Intercity_bus_service "Intercity bus service") transfer hub, served by seven carriers operating 12 routes.[[459]](#cite_note-461) [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s *[Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(passenger_train) "Cardinal (passenger train)")* passenger rail service operates three weekly roundtrips between [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). Located in the enclave of [Beech Grove](/wiki/Beech_Grove,_Indiana "Beech Grove, Indiana"), the [Beech Grove Shops](/wiki/Beech_Grove_Shops "Beech Grove Shops") serve as Amtrak's primary heavy maintenance and overhaul facility.[[460]](#cite_note-Bogle-462) About 282 miles (454 km)[[442]](#cite_note-transport-444) of freight rail lines traverse the city, including one [Class I](/wiki/Railroad_classes#Class_I "Railroad classes") railroad ([CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation")), one [Class II](/wiki/Railroad_classes#Class_II "Railroad classes") railroad ([Indiana Rail Road](/wiki/Indiana_Rail_Road "Indiana Rail Road")), and two [shortline railroads](/wiki/Shortline_railroad "Shortline railroad") ([Indiana Southern Railroad](/wiki/Indiana_Southern_Railroad "Indiana Southern Railroad") and [Louisville and Indiana Railroad](/wiki/Louisville_and_Indiana_Railroad "Louisville and Indiana Railroad")).

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Utilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Aerial_view_of_Geist_Reservoir_and_surrounding_housing_developments_in_Indianapolis_suburb_of_Fishers%2C_Indiana.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_Geist_Reservoir_and_surrounding_housing_developments_in_Indianapolis_suburb_of_Fishers%2C_Indiana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_Geist_Reservoir_and_surrounding_housing_developments_in_Indianapolis_suburb_of_Fishers,_Indiana.jpg)

[Geist Reservoir](/wiki/Geist_Reservoir "Geist Reservoir") in northeast Indianapolis is one of the region's four reservoirs.

[AES Indiana](/wiki/AES_Indiana "AES Indiana") supplies electricity to more than 500,000 Indianapolis customers[[461]](#cite_note-463) and maintains 90,000 street lights.[[462]](#cite_note-464) Natural gas, water, and wastewater utilities are provided by Citizens Energy Group.[[463]](#cite_note-465) The company's thermal division operates the [Perry K. Generating Station](/wiki/Perry_K._Generating_Station "Perry K. Generating Station") which produces and distributes steam for heating and cooling to about 160 customers in downtown Indianapolis.[[464]](#cite_note-466) The city's water supply is sourced from the [White River](/wiki/White_River_(Indiana) "White River (Indiana)") and its tributaries as well as [aquifers](/wiki/Aquifer "Aquifer") via four surface water treatment plants, four reservoirs, and five groundwater pumping stations throughout the region.[[465]](#cite_note-467)

[Area codes 317 and 463](/wiki/Area_codes_317_and_463 "Area codes 317 and 463") are telephone [area codes](/wiki/Area_code "Area code") in the [North American Numbering Plan](/wiki/North_American_Numbering_Plan "North American Numbering Plan") assigned to Indianapolis and seven surrounding counties in Central Indiana. Established in 1947, 317 is the original area code for the Indianapolis area, while 463 is an [overlay code](/wiki/Overlay_plan "Overlay plan") for the same area that was added in 2016, making [ten-digit dialing](/wiki/Ten-digit_dialing "Ten-digit dialing") mandatory for all calls in the region.[[466]](#cite_note-468) Telecommunications, including cable television, internet, telephone, and wireless services, are provided by [AT&T Communications](/wiki/AT%26T_Communications "AT&T Communications"), Metronet, [Spectrum](/wiki/Spectrum_(brand) "Spectrum (brand)"), [Verizon Communications](/wiki/Verizon_Communications "Verizon Communications"), and [Xfinity](/wiki/Xfinity "Xfinity").[[467]](#cite_note-469)

[Waste collection](/wiki/Waste_collection "Waste collection") services in Indianapolis are provided by the city's Department of Public Works Solid Waste Division, [Republic Services](/wiki/Republic_Services "Republic Services"), and [WM](/wiki/Waste_Management_(corporation) "Waste Management (corporation)").[[468]](#cite_note-470) Solid waste disposal in the city is processed by [landfill](/wiki/Landfill "Landfill") and [incineration](/wiki/Incineration "Incineration"). [Reworld](/wiki/Reworld "Reworld") operates a [waste-to-energy](/wiki/Waste-to-energy "Waste-to-energy") plant in the city. About 11% of residents subscribe to private [curbside recycling](/wiki/Curbside_recycling "Curbside recycling") services;[[469]](#cite_note-Russell2023-471) however, free public recycling drop-off sites are available throughout the city.[[470]](#cite_note-472) Of U.S. cities, Indianapolis is the largest without a universal curbside recycling program, resulting in one of the lowest [landfill diversion](/wiki/Landfill_diversion "Landfill diversion") rates.[[469]](#cite_note-Russell2023-471)[[471]](#cite_note-473)

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indianapolis&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

See also: [List of hospitals in Indianapolis](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Indianapolis "List of hospitals in Indianapolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Eskenazi_Hospital.jpg/250px-Eskenazi_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eskenazi_Hospital.jpg)

[Sidney & Lois Eskenazi Hospital](/wiki/Sidney_%26_Lois_Eskenazi_Hospital "Sidney & Lois Eskenazi Hospital"), the city's flagship [safety net hospital](/wiki/Safety_net_hospital "Safety net hospital")

Healthcare in Indianapolis is provided by about 20 hospitals, most belonging to the [private](/wiki/Private_hospital "Private hospital"), [non-profit](/wiki/Non-profit_hospital "Non-profit hospital") [hospital networks](/wiki/Hospital_network "Hospital network") of [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_(healthcare_system) "Ascension (healthcare system)") [St. Vincent Health](/wiki/St._Vincent_Health "St. Vincent Health"), Community Health Network,[[472]](#cite_note-474) and [IU Health](/wiki/IU_Health "IU Health").[[473]](#cite_note-475) Several are [teaching hospitals](/wiki/Teaching_hospitals "Teaching hospitals") affiliated with the [IU School of Medicine](/wiki/IU_School_of_Medicine "IU School of Medicine") or Marian University's [Tom and Julie Wood College of Osteopathic Medicine](/wiki/Tom_and_Julie_Wood_College_of_Osteopathic_Medicine "Tom and Julie Wood College of Osteopathic Medicine"). Four hospitals are [Level I trauma centers](/wiki/Trauma_center "Trauma center").[[474]](#cite_note-476)

Health and Hospital Corporation of Marion County oversees the city's public health facilities and programs, including the Marion County Public Health Department, Indianapolis Emergency Medical Services, and Eskenazi Health.[[475]](#cite_note-477) Eskenazi Health operates ten [primary care](/wiki/Primary_care "Primary care") sites across the city, including the [Sidney & Lois Eskenazi Hospital](/wiki/Sidney_%26_Lois_Eskenazi_Hospital "Sidney & Lois Eskenazi Hospital").[[476]](#cite_note-478) Other [public hospitals](/wiki/Public_hospital "Public hospital") include the Richard L. Roudebush VA Medical Center (managed by the [Veterans Health Administration](/wiki/Veterans_Health_Administration "Veterans Health Administration"))[[477]](#cite_note-479) and the [NeuroDiagnostic Institute](/wiki/NeuroDiagnostic_Institute "NeuroDiagnostic Institute") (managed by the State of Indiana).[[478]](#cite_note-480)

IU Health [Methodist](/wiki/Indiana_University_Health_Methodist_Hospital "Indiana University Health Methodist Hospital"), [University](/wiki/Indiana_University_Health_University_Hospital "Indiana University Health University Hospital"), and [Riley Hospital for Children](/wiki/Riley_Hospital_for_Children "Riley Hospital for Children") are affiliated with the IU School of Medicine. Riley is among the nation's foremost [children's hospitals](/wiki/Children%27s_hospital "Children's hospital"), recognized in all ten pediatric specialties by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[479]](#cite_note-481) IU Health is consolidating and replacing Methodist and University hospitals with a new $4.3 billion [academic medical center](/wiki/Academic_medical_center "Academic medical center") which is slated to open in 2027.[[480]](#cite_note-Methodist-482) Other major hospitals include [Ascension St. Vincent Hospital - Indianapolis](/wiki/St._Vincent_Indianapolis_Hospital "St. Vincent Indianapolis Hospital"), [Community Hospital East](/wiki/Community_Hospital_East "Community Hospital East"), [Community Hospital North](/wiki/Community_Hospital_North "Community Hospital North"), and [Franciscan Health Indianapolis](/wiki/Franciscan_Health_Indianapolis "Franciscan Health Indianapolis").

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-152)** Mean monthly maxima and minima (i.e. the expected highest and lowest temperature readings at any point during the year or given month) calculated based on data at said location from 1991 to 2020.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-153)** Official records for Indianapolis kept at downtown from February 1871 to December 1942, and at Indianapolis Int'l since January 1943. For more information, see [Threadex](http://threadex.rcc-acis.org)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-486)** Indianapolis has one former sister city, [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough,_Toronto "Scarborough, Toronto"), Ontario, Canada. The relationship was formally established in 1996 but dissolved following the 1998 [amalgamation of Toronto](/wiki/Amalgamation_of_Toronto "Amalgamation of Toronto").[[482]](#cite_note-484)[[483]](#cite_note-485)
